document.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Cúram Express Rules Reference Manual

root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Cúram Express Rules Reference Manual
root.summary: This node is the full Cúram 8.1.2 Cúram Express Rules Reference Manual. It introduces CER as a rule-based language and runtime for business calculations, with an editor and testing environment, and explains core concepts like rule sets, rule classes, objects, attributes, sessions, XML storage, validation, and runtime recalculation. Major topics include developing and testing rule sets, localizing and validating rules, generating documentation and coverage reports, handling data and time-based changes, Dependency Manager recalculation and batch/deferred processing, editor and palette references, best practices, XML dictionary elements, list operations, datastore integration, and compliancy rules. It also covers legal notices, privacy, and trademarks. Useful if you need the overall CER architecture, authoring workflow, runtime behavior, supported APIs/tables, or compliance constraints.

children.title: Note
children.summary: This note tells readers to review the Notices section before using the information or the supported product. It also includes the copyright notice for Merative US L.P. covering 2012 and 2024.

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Edition notice for Cúram 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2, followed by the start of “Cúram Express Rules Reference.” Introduces CER as a rule-based business calculation system with authoring, testing, runtime execution, and editor support. Covers core concepts (rule class, rule object, rule attribute, expression, rule set, rule session), production vs test usage, and key benefits such as simplicity, flexibility, localization, validity, testability, dynamic updates, and spreadsheet-like recalculation via Dependency Manager.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: Introduces CER’s role in eligibility, advisor, and custom calculations, then outlines core principles: on-demand computation, immutability, no execution order or working storage, and dependency-managed recalculation. Covers developing/testing rule sets with the CER editor, localization of calculated data and artifact descriptions, and multi-locale display rules. Also explains validating rule sets, running them via the interpreter, and using the test code generator for typed wrappers, with a warning that generated code is test-only and machine-specific.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-34
children.summary: Covers CER development utilities and documentation for pages 25–34: using generated test classes for HelloWorld rules, running the CER code generator, when to regenerate tests, reporting rule-set coverage, maintaining/publishing rule sets, generating schema/catalog files, and generating RuleDoc/SessionDoc. Includes sample XML and screenshots for simple and more complex RuleDoc examples, showing rule class/attribute navigation and dependency visualization.

children.title: Edition — pages 35-44
children.summary: Covers CER debugging and tooling for rule objects: generating SessionDoc for tests and stored objects, explaining SessionDoc fields (name, type, state, value, derivation, depends on, used by), and the DumpOutRuleObjects and snapshot extraction commands. Also includes a report for unused rule attributes and rule set consolidation with include handling plus an XML example. Begins data handling: CER sessions, rule objects vs attribute values, session factory, recalculation strategies, and data storage options (in-memory, database, snapshot, converters).

children.title: Edition — pages 45-54
children.summary: Covers CER rule object lifecycle and data typing. Explains storage/factory choices for unit tests, production, and snapshots; warns against multiple CER sessions per transaction. Describes external rule objects (bootstrap/top-level, created outside calculations, retrievable via readall) and internal rule objects (created by rules, not retrievable by readall, pooled within a session). Introduces rule class data types, inheritance, abstract rule classes, initialization inheritance, and validator checks for incompatible return types, with XML/code examples.

children.title: Edition — pages 55-64
children.summary: Covers CER data typing basics: the root rule class and default description attribute, using Java classes as data types, package-name rules, immutability requirements, inheritance and parameterized Java classes, and code table entry types. Also explains that every attribute needs a declared type and which expressions require explicit typing. The latter pages introduce Timeline data: what it is, when to use it, examples of temporal vs non-temporal data, and a point-in-time vs timeline perspective for rule design.

children.title: Edition — pages 65-74
children.summary: Explains how CER timeline data works and how to create it. Uses the “lone parent of a minor” example to show timeline-based reasoning over changing facts, then introduces Java and CER-rule methods for building Timeline values. Covers constructing timelines with Interval, using an artificial limit for age timelines, creating timelines directly in rules with Timeline/Interval, and the existencetimeline expression for start/end-date-based existence periods. Also notes that normal CER operations can be applied to timelines while preserving change dates.

children.title: Edition — pages 75-84
children.summary: Explains CER timeline handling and testing. Introduces date-shifting and date-spreading as business-specific timeline transformations implemented via static Java methods called from rules, with cautions about duplicate dates and edge cases. Gives examples: adding 3 months to benefit-reapplication bans and spreading car tax due dates to whole months. Then covers testing timeline outputs, recommending constant-forever timelines for simple tests, and shows strict vs relaxed assertions on resultant timeline values.

children.title: Edition — pages 85-94
children.summary: This section explains CER timeline properties and how interval values are derived from input timelines, including infinite coverage, immutability, no gaps, and merged intervals. It then introduces the Dependency Manager for recalculating CER results when precedents change: key concepts, dependency storage, sparse dependency matrices, optional storage, and granularity trade-offs. It also covers how precedent change items are captured into change sets, with deferred processing as the default queueing mode.

children.title: Edition — pages 95-104
children.summary: Explains Dependency Manager precedent change handling in deferred and batch modes: lifecycle of precedent change sets (open, submitted, complete), identifying and recalculating affected dependents, and the batch suite workflow. Covers deferred-processing limits, fallback to batch on overflow/error, optional hook for detecting deferred-to-batch transfer, and batch commands for submit, perform recalculations, and complete. Also notes dependent-type processing, streaming/parallel batch execution, logging/reporting, and SQL to find affected cases.

children.title: Edition — pages 105-114
children.summary: Covers CER–Dependency Manager integration and dependency storage. Explains how CER identifies precedents from calculated attribute trees, including stored attribute values, rule set groups/definitions, and readall/readall-match searches, plus how rule object converters add precedents. Includes an example tax liability dependency table and how precedent changes trigger recalculation of stored calculated attributes. The section then begins CER Editor Reference, describing the global menu, search criteria, business vs technical views, outline views, diagram canvas, pan/zoom, palettes, templates, and common rule/class/attribute actions.

children.title: Edition — pages 115-124
children.summary: This section catalogs CER editor palettes and rule elements, mainly for pages 115–124. It lists graphical rule elements for expression, data type, technical, household/financial/food assistance templates, and decision tables, with brief descriptions and page references. It also explains palette docking/expanding, rule element pop-up menus, property panels, rule class and attribute properties, and rule element wizards. From page 122 onward it begins detailed reference entries for core elements like Rule, Reference, And/Or groups, and Not, including properties and menu actions.

children.title: Edition — pages 125-134
children.summary: This section documents Cúram Express Rules palette elements and their menus/properties, covering logical, arithmetic, list, legislation, data type, technical logic, and fixed list/property constructs. It explains how elements like Choose, Compare, Arithmetic, Min/Max, Sum, Repeating Rule, Filter, Size, Legislation Change/Era, Boolean, String, Number, Date, Code Table, Rate, Frequency Pattern, Resource Message, Xml Message, Null, Create, Search, Fixed List, and Property behave, where they can be nested, and their editable properties and pop-up actions. Useful for understanding rule editor capabilities and element-specific configuration.

children.title: Edition — pages 135-144
children.summary: This section documents CER rule editor elements and their properties/menu actions: Custom Expression, Existence Timeline, Timeline, Interval, CombineSuccessionSet, Call, Period Length, ALL/ANY, This, Sort, Shared Rule Reference, Concat, Join Lists, and household/financial/food assistance templates. It also covers Decision Table setup and editing, then begins CER best practices, especially creating a meaningful description rule attribute. Important note: if a used property/static method implementation changes, create a new one and repoint rule sets so CER/Dependency Manager can recalculate stored values.

children.title: Edition — pages 145-154
children.summary: This section shows how CER rule-object descriptions work in practice, with an example rule set and test code demonstrating `toString()`/SessionDoc output. It then gives design guidance: use descriptions to distinguish instances, use shortcuts to get a rule set runnable quickly but keep tests, and name rule elements with business meaning and camelCase. It also covers when to use references, RuleDoc, inheritance vs containment, removing unused rules, declaration order effects, creation of rule objects, passing objects instead of IDs, and using static Java methods via `call`.

children.title: Edition — pages 155-164
children.summary: Covers CER static Java method use in rule sets: example of strongly typed vs weakly typed methods for finding a person’s most recent income, plus warnings that static/property methods must be deterministic and side-effect free. Also explains common JUnit test pitfalls: use CREOLETestHelper.assertEquals for numeric values, call .getValue() on AttributeValues, specify all needed inputs once, don’t specify values twice, and create all rule objects before calculations. Introduces the CER XML dictionary for RuleSet, Include, RuleClass, and Initialization elements.

children.title: Edition — pages 165-174
children.summary: Cúram CER rules reference covering attribute ordering, attribute structure, expression groupings, and detailed entries for marker and boolean-list expressions. It explains that initialization attributes should not be reordered, calculated attributes can be, and each attribute has a type and derivation. It introduces the alphabetical expression index, then defines the abstract marker, and the all/any boolean aggregations, including empty-list results, short-circuit behavior, error-reporting nuances, and performance guidance. Includes example rule sets and usage with fixedlist/dynamiclist.

children.title: Edition — pages 175-184
children.summary: Cúram Express Rules reference covering expression types: `any` behavior with error suppression in child expressions, `arithmetic` operations (+, -, *, /) and division rounding warnings, `call` to static Java methods and immutability cautions, `choose` conditional branching and ordering, `Code` for codetable entries, `compare` operators and supported types, plus `concat` for localized messages. Includes XML rule examples for each and notes on automatic recalculation when static methods change.

children.title: Edition — pages 185-194
children.summary: Cúram Express Rules Reference examples for core expressions: concat for building localized messages from mixed String/Date values; create for instantiating and pooling rule objects with initialization/specify syntax; current for list-processing context and aliases; Date literals and null dates; dynamiclist for mapping one list to another and using any; defaultDescription as root-rule-class only; equals with safe null handling and numeric normalization; existencetimeline for creating timelines from optional start/end dates.

children.title: Edition — pages 195-204
children.summary: Covers Cúram Express Rules expressions: existence timelines (using birth/death dates with pre/existence/post values), false, filter, fixedlist, FrequencyPattern, Interval, intervalvalue, joinlists, legislationChange, and max. Includes XML examples showing timeline construction, list filtering/joining, fixed lists, and literal constants. Useful for rule authors working with temporal values, list operations, and built-in literals/functions.

children.title: Edition — pages 205-214
children.summary: This section covers CER expressions for max, min, not, null, Number literals, periodlength, and property. It explains how max/min work on comparable lists, including dynamic lists and Number conversion to BigDecimal; how not negates Boolean values; how null is used in derivations; Number syntax including decimals and percentages; periodlength units and inclusive/exclusive end-date behavior; and property calls to safe Java methods, with examples and safe-list rules for Timeline, Object, Number, and List.

children.title: Edition — pages 215-224
children.summary: Cúram Express Rules Reference examples and guidance for CER expressions from pages 215–224. Covers the safe list for `java.util.List` methods, List property usage (`isEmpty`, `subList`), `readall` with optional `match` and `singleitem`, `reference` expressions, `removeduplicates`, and `ResourceMessage` for localized messages. Includes XML rule-set examples and notes on recalculation behavior for stored calculated attributes, plus performance guidance for `readall/match` versus filtering.

children.title: Edition — pages 225-234
children.summary: Cúram Express Rules Reference pages 225–234 covering expression examples and semantics for singleitem, sort, specified, String, sublists, sum, and this. It explains how singleitem handles empty/multiple lists, how sort orders list items with multiple sort keys, that specified marks externally supplied attributes, String literals in rule sets, sublists generating all subsets, sum aggregating fixed or dynamic lists (empty list = 0), and this referencing the current rule object. Includes XML rule examples for Person, Household, Asset, and Pet classes.

children.title: Edition — pages 235-244
children.summary: Covers CER expressions Timeline, timelineoperation, true, and XmlMessage, plus the Annotations framework. Timeline builds time-varying values and may need an initialvalue or a null-start interval. timelineoperation assembles a timeline by repeatedly evaluating a child expression at dates from intervalvalue-wrapped timelines; omitting intervalvalue yields a constant timeline and causes type errors in some cases. true returns Boolean true. XmlMessage creates localizable message XML with replace tokens and notes whitespace-trimming behavior changes. Annotations can be attached to rule sets, classes, attributes, and expressions; Label is shown with XML examples.

children.title: Edition — pages 245-254
children.summary: Covers several CER reference topics: annotations such as primary and tags, useful java.util.List safe operations in rules, and a detailed example of mapping Datastore records to CER rule objects via DataStoreRuleObjectCreator (including parentEntity and childEntities_<rule class name>). Also introduces CER compliancy guidance: supported APIs/expressions, restrictions on custom implementations/packages, included rule set constraints, and notes on internal database tables and CREOLERuleSet handling.

children.title: Edition — pages 255-258
children.summary: This section explains CER rule-set database and DMX handling. It describes the strict conditions for using DMX to populate CREOLERuleSet, the reduced validation compared with the CER API, snapshot/versioning behavior, and the recommendation to run creole.upload.rulesets and creole.validate.rulesets. It also identifies internal CER tables that must not be accessed directly, plus Dependency Manager compliancy notes and its own protected tables. Useful if you need ruleset import, validation, or database-table restrictions.

Edition notice for Cúram 8.1/8.1.1/8.1.2 and the start of the Cúram Express Rules Reference. The section introduces CER as a rule-based calculation language with authoring, testing, runtime, and editor support, then covers development and testing, data handling, timelines, Dependency Manager recalculation, editor reference, best practices, XML dictionary, list operations, datastore integration, and compliancy rules. It also details rule-set database/DMX constraints and protected internal CER/Dependency Manager tables.

It points to the Merative privacy policy at merative.com/privacy and lists trademarks for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft, and UNIX. Legal notices for using the publications: personal and enterprise use are allowed only with proprietary notices preserved, and redistribution/derivative works are restricted without Merative consent. It disclaims warranties, states the content is provided “AS-IS,” notes no patent license is granted, and warns that non-Merative product info and external websites are not endorsed or guaranteed. Also includes export-law compliance and copyright notice. Useful for license, warranty, and usage-rights reference.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the Cúram Express Rules Reference. Lists major sections on CER overview, developing/testing rule sets, handling data, recalculation with the Dependency Manager, editor reference, palette rule elements, best practices, XML dictionary, list operations, datastore use, and compliancy. Also includes notices, privacy policy, and trademarks. Useful as a navigation index for the full CER manual rather than a substantive technical section.

children.title: 1 Cúram Express Rules Reference
1.1 CER Overview
What is CER?
Rules Language
children.summary: Introduces CER as the rules language in Cúram Express Rules for business calculations. It defines questions, each with a name, answer data type, and rules for deriving the answer when asked. The section explains that answers can be simple (e.g., yes/no) or complex structures, and that rules may directly return values or depend on further questions. It also situates CER within the broader development and runtime environment, including the CER editor for creating and managing rule sets.

children.title: Authoring and Testing Environment
children.summary: CER rule sets are authored and maintained in the CER Editor as XML stored in the application database and conforming to the CER rule schema. The editor includes a validator to catch rule-set errors before execution, and rule sets can be validated from the CER Editor. CER rule sessions can run in either a production environment or a stand-alone test environment.

children.title: Runtime Environment
children.summary: This section explains the CER runtime and testing environment. It says CER runs rules on demand in production, supports dynamic rule-set updates without rebuilding or redeploying, and lets you test at different levels, including isolated tests by stubbing calculations. It also mentions development/testing tools such as RuleDoc, SessionDoc, and Coverage Tool, plus the Editor’s global menu. The section begins outlining runtime features such as storing rule objects in the database for future processing.

CER is the rules language, authoring environment, and runtime for Cúram business calculations. It defines questions with names, answer types, and rules that may return simple or complex results and can trigger further questions. Rule sets are created and maintained in the CER Editor as XML stored in the application database, validated before execution, and can run in production or a stand-alone test environment. CER supports dynamic rule updates without redeploying, isolated testing via stubs, and tools like RuleDoc, SessionDoc, and Coverage Tool.

children.title: Benefits of CER
children.summary: CER’s key benefits are simplicity, flexibility, localization support, validity, testability, dynamic support, and spreadsheet-like behavior. Rule sets are designed to be easy to read, write, and test; can be changed without affecting existing behavior; support localized output; are validated for errors before execution; can be tested in discrete sections; and can be updated in a running system with immediate effect. CER also recalculates dependent results automatically when input data changes.

children.title: CER Usage
children.summary: CER usage in Cúram: explains how CER is used by other application components and can also support custom business areas. It notes the CER development environment is tightly integrated with the broader application development environment. The excerpt begins listing application areas, including the Universal Access Responsive Web Application, which uses CER for self-service screening to determine potential eligibility for social services programs from captured user data.

children.title: Guiding Principles
children.summary: This section introduces CER’s guiding principles: questions are answered only when needed, results are immutable, multiple questions can be asked against a rule set, and users specify rules rather than execution order. It also emphasizes deterministic behavior with no volatility and no working storage/counters; counts and totals must be modeled as questions. Useful for understanding the core design philosophy behind CER rule evaluation.

Overview of Cúram Express Rules (CER): what it is, how it works, its benefits, where it is used, and its guiding principles. Covers CER as a rules language, authoring/test environment, and runtime for business calculations; core terminology like rule classes, objects, attributes, expressions, rule sets, and sessions; XML-based rule storage and validation; production and stand-alone testing with stubbing, RuleDoc/SessionDoc/Coverage Tool; dynamic updates and dependency-driven recalculation; usage in applications such as Universal Access, Advisor, and eligibility engines; and principles like immutability, no volatility, and no working storage.

children.title: 1.2 Developing and Testing CER Rule Sets
CER Rules Editor
children.summary: The CER Rules Editor is a user-friendly environment for business and technical users to create, edit, and validate CER rule sets and rule classes. This section also sits within the broader discussion of developing and testing CER rule sets, including localization, validation, running rules with the rule set interpreter, generating test code, publishing rule sets, and generating documentation. It points readers to the CER Editor Reference for more detail.

children.title: Localizing CER Rules
Localizing Calculated Data
children.summary: Explains how CER localizes calculated data and rule-set descriptions. Rule element names (classes, attributes, identifiers) cannot be localized, but descriptions can. Shows replacing hard-coded String output with curam.creole.value.Message using ResourceMessage and locale-specific properties files, then converting at runtime with toLocale(Locale). Notes localized formatting support for rule object descriptions, curam.util.type.Date, code table items, and nested messages; other objects use toString.

children.title: Localizing CER Rule Artifact Descriptions
children.summary: Explains how to localize CER rule artifact descriptions using the Label annotation. Supported artifacts include Rule Set, Rule Class, Rule Attribute, and Expression. Published rule sets store descriptions as resource-store property files that can be localized like other resources. Also covers multi-locale support for CER display rules via the curam.display.rules.multi.locale application property, enabling runtime language switching for translatable decision details and display rule content. Includes an example ResourceMessage/Message-based Rule Class description snippet.

Explains how CER supports localization for calculated data and rule artifact descriptions. Rule element names remain non-localizable identifiers, but descriptions can be localized. Shows replacing hard-coded String outputs with curam.creole.value.Message and ResourceMessage plus locale-specific properties files, then converting at runtime with toLocale(Locale). Notes locale-aware formatting for rule object descriptions, dates, code table items, and nested messages. Also covers Label-based descriptions for Rule Set/Class/Attribute/Expression, published resource-store property files, and multi-locale display rules via curam.display.rules.multi.locale.

children.title: Validating Rule Sets
children.summary: Explains how to validate CER rule sets using either the Administration Application or the CER editor. For file-system rule sets, use `build creole.validate.rulesets`, which runs the validator and reports errors and warnings. Notes that warnings are non-fatal but should be addressed for optimal rule sets.

children.title: Using the Rule Set Interpreter
children.summary: Explains how to use the CER rule set interpreter to run dynamic rule sets, with a HelloWorld example. Shows two execution options: as a stand-alone Java app or JUnit test. Covers reading and validating a rule set from XML, starting a CER session with `InterpretedRuleObjectFactory` and in-memory storage, creating a rule object instance, and accessing an attribute value by string name to execute rules.

children.title: Using the CER Test Code Generator
Generating Code
children.summary: Explains how to run the CER code generator to generate test classes using `build creole.generate.test.classes`, which also validates rule sets and stops on errors while reporting warnings. Notes the generated Java output location and shows a sample generated interface for the HelloWorld rule class. Also gives guidance on when to regenerate test classes: after adding/removing rule sets or rule classes, but not after changing rule attribute derivations, since derivations are evaluated dynamically at runtime.

Explains CER test code generation for strongly typed Java wrappers around rule classes and attributes. Shows using generated classes with `StronglyTypedRuleObjectFactory` to avoid string-based lookups and casts in interpreted tests. To generate, run `build creole.generate.test.classes`, which first validates rule sets, stops on errors, and reports warnings. Output is written to `EJBServer/build/svr/creole.gen/source`. Regenerate after adding/removing rule sets or rule classes, but not after changing attribute derivations, since they are evaluated dynamically at runtime.

children.title: Reporting on Rule Set Coverage
children.summary: Explains how to generate and use CER rule set coverage reports. It describes enabling runtime coverage logging via the curam.creole.coverage.logfile property in Bootstrap.properties, notes that coverage data is collected during application runs and JUnit tests, and gives the build target to create the report. The report is color-coded and drillable: green covered, yellow partially covered, red not covered. It also notes how to clear or disable logging and that <specified> derivation attributes are excluded.

children.title: Maintaining and Publishing Rule Sets
children.summary: This section explains how the Administration Application is used to maintain CER rule sets: view historical versions, edit existing sets, create new ones, remove sets, and synchronize edited sets with the file system or source control. It notes that changes are held in a publication area, can be validated before publishing, and are applied via deferred processing that may update rule objects or queued recalculations. It also covers generating the CER schema and catalog files for validation, including the build commands and output locations.

children.title: Generating the Rule Set Schema and Catalog
children.summary: Explains how CER rule sets are maintained and published in the Administration Application, including viewing, editing, creating, removing, validating, and deferred publication of changes. Notes that included rule sets become part of the outermost source for coverage analysis. Also describes generating the CER rule set schema and catalog on the file system: the schema is dynamically assembled for validation, and commands are provided to create RuleSet.xsd and CREOLECatalog.xml.

children.title: Generating Rule Documentation
Generating RuleDoc
Simple RuleDoc Example
children.summary: Explains how to generate CER RuleDoc from rule sets and rule classes using `build creole.generate.ruledoc`, which also runs the rule set validator and stops on errors. Notes that warnings are non-fatal but should be addressed, and that output is placed in `EJBServer/build/svr/creole.gen/ruledoc`. Includes a simple HelloWorldRuleSet XML example and describes the resulting RuleDoc UI: a rule set with one rule class, a class view, and an attribute (`greeting`) linking to its derivation.

children.title: More Complex RuleDoc Example
children.summary: Shows a more complex RuleDoc example for a retirement year calculator rule set. It explains how RuleDoc helps navigate rule-class dependencies, understand derivation calculations, and see which attributes depend on others. The section includes XML for a `RetirementYearRuleSet` with `yearOfBirth`, `ageAtRetirement`, and derived `yearOfRetirement = yearOfBirth + ageAtRetirement`, plus the generated RuleDoc illustrating derivation and usage links.

Explains how to generate CER RuleDoc for rule sets and rule classes using `build creole.generate.ruledoc`, which also runs validation and stops on errors; warnings are non-fatal but should be fixed. Output is written to `EJBServer/build/svr/creole.gen/ruledoc`. Includes a simple HelloWorld example and a more complex retirement-year calculator example showing how RuleDoc visualizes rule-class navigation, derivations, and attribute dependencies/usage links.

children.title: Generating SessionDoc
children.summary: Explains how to generate SessionDoc for debugging rule executions. Covers writing SessionDoc from tests via JUnit tearDown, what the main SessionDoc page shows, and how to drill into rule sets and rule objects. Describes the fields listed for each rule attribute: name, declared type, state, value, derivation, depends on, and used by, plus state meanings. Notes suppressing used-by links for large databases, using DumpOutRuleObjects for stored rule objects, and commands to extract case/program group determination snapshots to HTML.

Explains how to generate CER RuleDoc and SessionDoc for rule sets/classes. RuleDoc is built with `build creole.generate.ruledoc`, which also validates rules, stops on errors, warns on non-fatal issues, and writes output to `EJBServer/build/svr/creole.gen/ruledoc`. It includes simple and complex examples showing navigation, derivations, and attribute dependencies. SessionDoc is a debugging aid for tests and stored rule objects, covering creation via JUnit tearDown, attribute fields/state meanings, suppressing “used by” links, DumpOutRuleObjects, and commands to extract case/program group determination snapshots to HTML.

children.title: Reporting on Unused Rule Attributes
children.summary: Explains how to generate a CER report of rule attributes not referenced by other calculations, using `build creole.report.unused.attributes`, and warns that top-level attributes used only by client code may be falsely reported as unused. Also introduces rule set consolidation: CER can split rule sets across files, and the build target `creole.consolidate.rulesets` merges them into one file before loading, collapsing only `Include` statements with `RelativePath` locations.

children.title: Consolidating Rule Sets
children.summary: Explains CER rule set consolidation and unused-attribute reporting. It warns that “unused” attributes may still be referenced by client code or tests, so they should not be removed without verification. The section gives the command to run the unused attribute report (`build creole.report.unused.attributes`) and notes that CER validates rule sets while reporting. It also says build target `creole.consolidate.rulesets` merges split rule set files, but only collapses `Include` statements with `RelativePath`; other include types stay unchanged. Includes a before/after XML example.

Covers CER rule set development and testing: using the CER editor, localizing calculated values and artifact descriptions, validating file-system or editor-based rule sets, running rules with the interpreter, generating strongly typed test wrappers, producing coverage reports, publishing/maintaining rule sets and schema/catalog files, generating RuleDoc and SessionDoc for debugging, reporting unused attributes, and consolidating split rule sets. Includes build commands, runtime localization behavior, multi-locale display rules, and notes on warnings, historical determinations, and include handling.

children.title: 1.3 Handling Data
Creating CER Sessions
children.summary: Explains how to create CER sessions with `Session_Factory`, defining the core session inputs: recalculation strategy, data storage, and rule object factory. Describes available strategies (`RecalculationsProhibited`, immediate, deferred), storage options (`InMemoryDataStorage`, `DatabaseDataStorage`, `SnapshotDataStorage`, `HybridDataStorage`), and factories (`StronglyTypedRuleObjectFactory`, `InterpretedRuleObjectFactory`). Notes database/snapshot behavior, rule object converters, and warns against multiple CER sessions per transaction. Includes recommended combinations for unit tests, production dynamic rule sets, and snapshots.

children.title: Creating External and Internal Rule Objects
Creating External Rule Objects
children.summary: Explains external vs internal rule objects in CER and when to use external objects as bootstrap/top-level entities like Person or Case. Notes that external objects must exist before calculations, can be retrieved via readall, and creation after getValue is prohibited by RecalculationsProhibited. Includes an XML example with Person and Income classes and shows client code/test creation using a session, generated factories or interpreter, and in-memory storage.

children.title: Creating Internal Rule Objects
children.summary: Explains how CER rules can create internal rule objects with the create expression, either as intermediate calculation steps, conditionally, or with calculated initialization values. Shows how created objects can be referenced by other calculations, including anonymous creation inside another expression, but not retrieved with readall. Notes that create is evaluated only when needed. Also covers internal rule object pooling in a session: identical created objects are reused for efficiency, with safe reuse because outputs depend only on inputs.

Explains external vs internal CER rule objects. External objects are bootstrap/top-level entities (for example Person or Case) created outside calculations, available to readall, and must be created before any getValue because RecalculationsProhibited blocks later creation. Includes XML and Java/test examples using sessions and factories. Internal objects are created with create during rule evaluation, can be conditional or anonymous, are only computed when needed, cannot be retrieved with readall, and are pooled within a session so identical created objects are reused safely.

children.title: Handling Data Types
Rule Classes
children.summary: Explains CER rule classes as a data type within the same rule set, with an example of one rule class referencing another. Covers simple implementation inheritance for rule classes, including `extends`, inherited calculated and initialized attributes, overriding calculations, and initialization ordering for ancestors. Notes that abstract attributes force a class to be abstract, abstract classes cannot be created directly, and rule objects can be used where ancestor types are expected. Includes validator behavior for incompatible returns and an inheritance example.

children.title: Java Classes
children.summary: Explains how CER rule sets use Java classes as data types. Covers fully qualified package names (except java.lang and java.util), the need for immutable classes, and common supported immutable types. Shows that CER respects Java inheritance, so subtype values can satisfy ancestor type expectations, and validates mismatches. Also introduces parameterized Java classes, including lists of strings, numbers, rule classes, and nested rule-class lists.

children.title: Code Tables
children.summary: This section explains how CER uses application code tables as a data type in rule sets, including dynamically created tables. It shows how to create a code table entry value with the Code expression and gives an XML example using a codetableentry attribute and comparison against a Gender code table value. It also notes that every attribute, whether calculated or initialized, must have a specified data type.

children.title: Where to Specify Data Types
children.summary: This section says every attribute, whether calculated or initialized, must have a data type. It also notes that CER supports any application code table as a data type, including code tables created later by administrators, and explains using the Code expression to create a code table entry value. It reiterates strong typing and inheritance handling for dynamically defined rule classes.

Explains CER data typing for rule sets: every calculated or initialized attribute must declare a type. Covers rule class types within the same rule set, inheritance (`extends`), abstract attributes/classes, initialization order, and type compatibility. Describes Java class types, requiring fully qualified names outside `java.lang`/`java.util`, immutable classes, inheritance, and parameterized classes such as lists. Also covers application code tables as types, including dynamic tables and the `Code` expression for code table entries.

children.title: Handling Data that Changes Over Time
What Is Timeline Data?
children.summary: Defines CER timeline data as a sequence of values of one type, each effective from a date until replaced. Emphasizes using Timeline only when a data item can vary over time. Gives examples: income as Timeline<Number>, primary employment as Timeline<Employment>, car ownership and alive/dead status as Timeline<Boolean>, and children as Timeline<List<Person>>. Also distinguishes that a value exists for any given date and notes timeline notation borrows from Java generics.

children.title: Comparing Timeline and Point-in-time Perspectives
Example of point-in-time perspective
children.summary: Shows a point-in-time derivation example for the rule “lone parent of a minor”: a person is a lone parent on a given date only if not married and has a dependent under 16. Uses a truth table and a scenario with Mary, Joe, and James to evaluate status on specific dates around marriage, divorce, and Joe’s death. Useful for understanding temporal rule evaluation and how changing circumstances affect date-based eligibility.

children.title: Example of Timeline Perspective
children.summary: The section reworks the “lone parent of a minor” rule into a timeline perspective: a person is such a parent whenever they are not married and have a dependent under 16. It explains that instead of checking isolated dates, you build timelines for marriage and dependent-under-16 status for Joe, Mary, and James, then derive their lone-parent status over time. It highlights that timeline-based evaluation helps identify when status changes and why some dates matter while others do not.

Explains how to think about CER rules either at a single date or over time. The section contrasts a point-in-time evaluation of “lone parent of a minor” with a timeline-based view. The point-in-time example uses Mary, Joe, and James to test status on specific dates around marriage, divorce, and Joe’s death, showing how outcomes can change with date selection. The timeline example rewords the rule as “whenever” and builds timelines for marriage and dependent-under-16 status to derive lone-parent status over time.

children.title: Creating Timelines
Creating Timelines in Java Code
children.summary: Explains how to create timeline data in Java using `curam.creole.value.Timeline` and `Interval`. Shows that a timeline is built from a collection of intervals passed to the constructor, with examples for a `Timeline<Number>` spanning open-ended time ranges. Also includes callable sample code for CER that creates an age timeline from date of birth, limited to 200 birthdays, using `Session`, `Date`, `Calendar.roll`, and interval merging behavior.

children.title: Creating Timelines in CER Rules
children.summary: Explains how to create timeline data directly in CER rules, though it is usually created outside rules by CER clients and populated via the specify mechanism. The section introduces two relevant expressions: Timeline (used with Interval) and existencetimeline. It shows how to build a native Timeline by defining a list of intervals, with examples for fixed timelines, including use of an initialvalue from the start of time and the requirement for a null-date/start-of-time interval when no initialvalue is provided.

Explains how timeline data is created in CER. In Java, timelines are built as `curam.creole.value.Timeline` objects from a collection of `Interval`s, with examples for open-ended numeric timelines and an age timeline callable from CER that uses `Session`, `Date`, and `Calendar.roll`. In CER rules, timelines are usually populated externally but can be created directly using `Timeline` with `Interval` or `existencetimeline`. Shows fixed timeline syntax, use of `<initialvalue>`, and the need for a start-of-time interval if no initial value is provided.

children.title: Operating on Timelines
Preserving change dates
children.summary: Explains how CER expressions preserve timeline change dates when operating on timeline values: input change points map naturally to output change points, so ordinary expressions can work on Timeline types as well as primitive values. Introduces special expressions like timelineoperation and intervalvalue that hide timeline handling from other expressions. Uses sum over income timelines as an example of deriving a changing total income over time.

children.title: Date-shifting
children.summary: Explains date-shifting in CER by using static Java methods called from rules, since CER has no built-in date-shift expressions. Covers two patterns: date addition and date spreading. Shows how to derive a new timeline from another by shifting value-change dates, with warnings about avoiding duplicate dates and testing edge cases like leap years. Includes sample Java implementations: adding months to benefit timelines and spreading “on-road” periods to whole-month tax liability timelines.

Explains CER timeline operations and how expressions can apply to Timeline values without changing change-date semantics. Covers special helpers like timelineoperation and intervalvalue, and uses sum to compute a total-income timeline from changing incomes. Also introduces business-specific date shifting via static Java methods called from rules, with warnings about duplicate dates and edge cases. Gives examples of date addition (benefit reapply delay) and date spreading (car tax due for any month with on-road days).

children.title: Testing Timeline Outputs
children.summary: Explains how to test timeline outputs in JUnit for Cúram rules. Recommends using constant-valued input timelines to simplify tests, since you usually don’t need to verify change-date accumulation. Shows an example summing multiple `Timeline<Number>` inputs and asserting a constant result. Then outlines two approaches when exact timeline behavior matters: strict checking (compare whole timelines for equality) and relaxed checking (assert values on selected dates). Includes code examples for both styles.

children.title: Timeline Properties
Example of Timeline Intervals
children.summary: Explains how CER builds timeline intervals by evaluating input timeline change dates and merging adjacent intervals with the same result. Uses Mary’s `isLoneParentOfMinorTimeline` as the example, driven by `isMarriedTimeline` and `hasMinorDependentsTimeline`. Shows the relevant change dates (1 Jan 2001, 1 May 2004, 1 Jun 2006), applies boolean logic at each point, and demonstrates that identical start-of-time and 1 Jan 2001 values are combined into one interval.

Explains core CER Timeline Properties: timelines are immutable, parameterized by value type, and extend infinitely into the past and future with a value on every date. Values are stored as contiguous intervals; adjacent intervals with equal values are merged using Object.equals, so there are no gaps or natural start/end dates. A timeline must have a start-of-time value and can only change finitely many times, requiring sensible defaults for open-ended periods. Also includes an example showing how timeline intervals are derived from input change dates.

Explains CER timelines for data that changes over time: what qualifies as timeline data vs non-timeline data, and how to think in point-in-time versus timeline terms using the lone-parent example. Covers creating timelines in Java or CER, operating on timelines, and testing outputs with strict or relaxed assertions. Details core properties: immutable, typed, infinite past/future with a value on every date, contiguous merged intervals, no gaps or natural end dates, finite change points, and start-of-time value required.

children.title: Triggering Recalculation When Data Changes
children.summary: Explains that CER recalculation should be handled through the Dependency Manager rather than CER recalculation strategies. It describes how CER and related clients integrate with the Dependency Manager to recalculate results when input data changes, and defines core concepts: dependents, precedents, dependencies, dependency/precedent change items, precedent change sets, and dependent recalculation. Useful for understanding how changed inputs trigger affected result updates.

Covers CER data handling: sessions, rule objects, attribute values, and data storage/factory choices. Explains session creation with recalculation strategies (mostly deprecated in favor of Dependency Manager), storage options (in-memory, database, snapshot, hybrid), and recommended combinations for tests, production, and snapshots. Describes external vs internal rule objects and creation rules. Details data typing for rule classes, Java classes, and code tables. Also explains timelines for changing data, including strict vs relaxed testing and core timeline properties like immutability, infinite coverage, and merged intervals.

children.title: 1.4 Recalculating CER Results with the Dependency Manager
Dependency Manager Concepts
children.summary: Introduces Dependency Manager concepts used by CER recalculation. Defines dependents, precedents, dependencies, dependency/precedent change items, precedent change sets, and dependent recalculation, plus required identification by type and identifier. Explains with a benefit entitlement example and sparse dependency matrix how precedents map to affected dependents. Shows that when a precedent changes, only impacted dependents are recalculated, and multiple changes can be grouped into one change set with duplicates removed to minimize work.

children.title: Dependency Manager Database Considerations
children.summary: Explains how Dependency Manager dependency/precedent records are stored in the database. Each record uses a type code and a long string identifier (up to 1000 chars), with examples for entity rows, stored attribute values, active evidence, and rule sets. Because identifier queries are frequent, an integer hashcode is also stored and indexed for performance; it is auto-populated on the Dependency and PrecedentChangeItem tables. Also outlines core Dependency Manager functions: storing dependency records, capturing precedent changes, identifying affected dependents, and controlling recalculation.

children.title: Dependency Manager Functions
Storage of Dependency Records
Example of Storage of Dependency Records
children.summary: Explains how the Dependency Manager creates, updates, and removes dependency records during case entitlement recalculation. It shows initial storage of dependencies, adding new records when a household member is added, removing extraneous records when a member is removed, and deleting all records when a case closes, with recreation on reopen. Also emphasizes that the manager stores only dependency links, not full sets of dependents or precedents, to avoid duplication and bottlenecks, and relies on registered handlers for type-specific processing.

children.title: No Understanding of Dependents or Precedents
children.summary: Explains that the Dependency Manager stores only dependency links, not complete lists of all dependents or precedents. It avoids duplicating system data and potential concurrency bottlenecks. The section uses entitlement recalculation examples to show how dependency records are added, retained, removed, or cleared when cases close and reopen. It also notes that the manager does not interpret dependency data itself; instead, registered handlers for each dependent/precedent type perform business-specific processing.

children.title: Dependency Storage is Optional
children.summary: Dependency Manager storage is optional: clients can choose whether dependency records are needed for automatic identification and recalculation of dependents. The section contrasts cases that require stored dependencies (e.g., eligibility/entitlement determinations that must trigger reassessment) with manual calculations that do not. It also explains dependency granularity trade-offs: very fine granularity improves precision but increases record volume, while coarse granularity reduces storage but may cause unnecessary recalculations. Designers must choose an appropriate level for storing dependency information.

children.title: Granularity of Dependencies
children.summary: The Dependency Manager is agnostic about dependency meaning; clients must assign semantics and choose the right storage granularity. The section contrasts very fine granularity, which improves precision but greatly increases dependency records, with very coarse granularity, which reduces records but can trigger unnecessary recalculations. It emphasizes that designers must balance accuracy against storage and recalculation cost, using examples like personal details fields such as date of birth and number of children in entitlement calculations.

The section explains how the Dependency Manager stores dependency records: it creates and removes records only, never updates them, and clients must supply the precedents used in a calculation. It compares new dependencies with stored ones, adds or deletes rows as needed, and can clear all records when a case closes. It also notes zero-ID write rules, that the manager stores only dependency links (not full dependency/precedent lists), and that storage is optional. Finally, it covers dependency granularity trade-offs between precision and storage/recalculation cost.

children.title: Capture of Precedent Change Items
Queue for Deferred Processing
children.summary: Explains the default “queue for deferred processing” mode in the Dependency Manager for precedent changes. When precedent change items are found during a database transaction, it creates one new precedent change set, adds all items from that transaction, and queues a deferred process to handle it later. This follows the broader discussion of dependency granularity and how only changed precedent values should trigger recalculation.

children.title: Queue for Batch Processing
children.summary: Describes the “Queue for Batch Processing” mode for precedent changes in Cúram Dependency Manager. It is used when changes may trigger many dependent recalculations. Instead of immediate/deferred processing, precedent change items from transactions are added to a special system-wide batch precedent change set, and an informational log prompts an administrator to schedule batch processing. Includes an example of accumulating rule/rate changes and an overview of precedent change set lifecycle states: Open, Submitted, and Complete.

children.title: Example of Precedent Change Processing
children.summary: Explains how the Dependency Manager handles precedent changes in batch mode and contrasts it with deferred processing. It describes a system-wide batch precedent change set that accumulates changes across transactions, logs an admin message, and is later processed in batch to reassess affected dependents. The example shows both personal detail changes and published CER/rate changes triggering reassessment. It also introduces the precedent change set lifecycle: Open, Submitted, and Complete, with mode-dependent transitions.

children.title: Lifecycle of a Precedent Change Set
children.summary: Describes the lifecycle of a precedent change set in Cúram Express Rules: Open, Submitted, and Complete. Explains that Open allows adding new precedent change items, Submitted begins dependent-identification processing and closes the set to further changes, and Complete means recalculation of affected dependents is finished and the set is retained for history. Notes that transitions vary by capture mode, including deferred and batch processing, and that batch mode accumulates changes across transactions for later processing.

Explains how Cúram Dependency Manager captures precedent change items and stores them in precedent change sets for later processing. Covers default queue for deferred processing, batch processing for large-scale changes, and how notifications are typically sent by entity/rule propagators. Includes an example of personal detail and CER/rate changes triggering reassessment, plus the precedent change set lifecycle: Open, Submitted, Complete, with mode-specific transitions.

children.title: Identification of Potentially Affected Dependents
children.summary: Explains how Dependency Manager identifies potentially affected dependents after capturing precedent change items into a change set. It examines stored dependency records for each precedent, filters duplicates, and does this in either deferred or batch processing mode. Examples show that multiple row or mixed rule/rate changes still identify each case only once. It then requests recalculation of all identified dependents via registered handlers, which know the correct dependent-specific action (for example, reassessing a case).

children.title: Recalculation of Identified Dependents
children.summary: Explains how Dependency Manager recalculates dependents after identifying them as potentially affected by precedent changes. Recalculation happens in deferred or batch processing, depending on capture mode. Dependency Manager does not know dependent-specific logic; it looks up a registered handler for each dependent and delegates recalculation to that handler. Example: a case assessment handler reassesses the case. Also notes that duplicate dependents are filtered out during identification, so each affected dependent is recalculated only once.

This section explains the Dependency Manager’s core functions: storing dependency records, capturing precedent change items, identifying potentially affected dependents, and triggering recalculation. It covers database representation (type plus identifier, backed by hashcodes), how records are only created or removed, zero-ID write rules, optional storage, and dependency granularity trade-offs. It also describes deferred vs batch processing, precedent change set lifecycle, duplicate-dependent filtering, and handler-based recalculation delegation.

children.title: Dependency Manager Deferred Processing
Setting the System Limit for Deferred Processing
children.summary: Explains how Dependency Manager deferred processing decides whether to recalculate dependent items immediately or move them to batch based on a configurable system limit. The limit is set via `curam.dependency.deferred.processing.limit` (default 50) and should be tuned by system testing. Also covers error handling: after retries, failed deferred work is transferred to batch and marked “Deferred To Batch,” with notifications sent. Describes an optional Java hook to detect deferred-to-batch transfers via Guice registration and an interface implementation.

children.title: Detecting transfer from Deferred Processing to Batch
children.summary: Explains how Dependency Manager deferred processing can transfer precedent change items to batch when the affected dependents exceed a configurable limit or after processing errors. Describes the system property curam.dependency.deferred.processing.limit (default 50), the Deferred To Batch status, and error-handling behavior. Also shows how to detect this transfer with the optional PrecedentChangeSetToBatchNotification hook, including Guice registration and a Java implementation. Notes the hook runs inside the transfer transaction and must not throw exceptions.

Explains Dependency Manager deferred processing: it first counts affected dependents and either recalculates them immediately or moves the precedent change set to batch if the configurable limit is exceeded (`curam.dependency.deferred.processing.limit`, default 50). Covers error handling: after retries, failed deferred work is also sent to batch and marked “Deferred To Batch,” with a notification to the originator. Describes an optional Java hook (`PrecedentChangeSetToBatchNotification`) to detect deferred-to-batch transfers, including Guice registration and an implementation that must not throw exceptions.

children.title: Dependency Manager Batch Processing
Submitting a Precedent Change Set
children.summary: Describes the batch suite start point for submitting the currently open precedent change set. Explains that it is a lightweight single-stream process that closes the open set and creates a new open set for later precedent changes. Gives the batch command to run, notes the success message, and warns about the common error of running it while another change set remains submitted. Also mentions that it outputs registered dependent types for the next batch step.

children.title: Performing Batch Recalculations From Precedent Change Set
children.summary: Describes the batch process that identifies and recalculates dependents affected by a submitted precedent change set. Explains it must be run once per registered dependent type, can be ordered or spread over days, and is blocked until the current change set completes. Covers the two phases (Identify Chunks and Process Chunks), normal and stream batch commands, failure conditions, result statuses (success/not found/error), reporting options including product-level output, and verbose logging for tracing recalculation triggers.

children.title: Completing a Precedent Change Set
children.summary: Explains the final batch step for a precedent change set: a lightweight single-stream process that completes the currently submitted set. It warns not to run it until “Perform Batch Recalculations From Precedent Change Set” has finished for every dependent type registered with the Dependency Manager. The section also notes that accidental reruns for the same dependent type are harmless but waste processing time, and provides SQL to identify affected cases with or without dependency hash code support.

Explains Dependency Manager batch processing for precedent change sets. Covers the three-step suite: submit the current open set, run batch recalculations once per registered dependent type, then complete the submitted set. Notes batch control records, queue-for-batch notifications, required commands, and common failure conditions. Describes streaming phases (identify/process chunks), result statuses, product-level reporting, verbose logging, and that reruns for the same dependent type are harmless but wasteful. Includes SQL to identify affected cases and precedents.

children.title: Integration between CER and the Dependency Manager
CER Utility to Identify Dependencies to Store
Precedents Identified Directly by CER
children.summary: Explains precedents that CER identifies directly from calculated attribute values for dependency storage: stored attribute values, rule set groups/definitions, and readall/readall-match searches. It describes how CER derives precedents from the logical dependency tree, ignoring intermediate values, and how changes to those precedents trigger recalculation. Includes a tax liability example showing stored dependencies for Joe and Mary and how asset changes, new assets, removals, and new tax thresholds produce precedent change items and recalculations.

children.title: Precedents Identified by Rule Object Converters
children.summary: This section explains how CER’s rule object converters can add dependency precedents, beyond the utility’s own identification. It describes storing dependencies for attribute values on CER database rule objects: derived values are dependents, input values are precedents. CER registers precedent and dependent handlers with the Dependency Manager so stored calculations can be tracked and recalculated. Example: a change to Income.amount can trigger recalculation of Person.totalIncome.

Explains CER’s utility for identifying precedents to store in the Dependency Manager from a calculated attribute’s dependency tree. CER directly derives precedents from stored attribute values, rule set groups/definitions, and readall/readall-match searches, ignoring intermediate values. Includes a tax-liability example showing stored dependencies for Joe and Mary and how asset value changes, asset additions/removals/transfers, and new tax thresholds create precedent change items that trigger recalculation.

children.title: Storing Dependencies for Attribute Values
children.summary: Explains how CER stores dependency relationships for attribute values in its database. Stored attribute values can act as dependents or precedents, with CER registering dependent and precedent handlers in the Dependency Manager. Example: Person.totalIncome may depend on stored Income.amount values. When a precedent changes, the Dependency Manager identifies affected stored values and asks CER to recalculate them. Also notes that rule object converters can contribute additional dependencies.

children.title: Recalculating Stored Calculated Attribute Values
children.summary: Explains how CER recalculates stored calculated attribute values when precedent data changes. The Dependency Manager tracks stored dependencies between precedents and dependents on CER database-stored rule objects, using registered dependent and precedent handlers. When a precedent changes, CER identifies affected dependents and recalculates them. Examples include tax liability recalculation when tax thresholds or rule sets change, and a Person.totalIncome value depending on Income.amount values. Also notes rule object converters can add dependency information.

CER integrates with the Dependency Manager in three ways: it identifies precedents to store, stores dependencies for calculated attribute values on CER database tables, and is called back to recalculate those values when precedents change. The section explains CER’s dependency-tree utility, which keeps only meaningful leaf precedents plus rule set/group and readall/readall-match dependencies, ignoring intermediate values. A tax-liability example shows how asset, threshold, and rule-set changes trigger recalculation for Joe and Mary, and notes rule object converters can add dependencies.

Explains how CER uses the Dependency Manager to recalculate stored results when inputs change. Covers core terms (dependent, precedent, dependency, precedent change set), sparse dependency storage, type+identifier records with hashcodes, and how only impacted dependents are recalculated. Describes dependency storage/update rules, deferred vs batch processing, and CER’s integration for stored attribute values, rule sets, readall/readall-match searches, and rule object converters. Includes a tax-liability example showing which changes trigger recalculation.

children.title: 1.5 CER Editor Reference
CER Editor Global Menu
Global Menu Functions
children.summary: Describes the CER editor’s Global menu functions: Undo/Redo, Include Rule Sets, Export diagrams to PNG ZIP, Save/Save All for rule sets in different statuses, Validate with the CER rule-set validator and error reporting, and Search for text matches. Useful if you need editor commands for managing, exporting, saving, validating, or finding rule sets.

children.title: Search Criteria
children.summary: This section explains search criteria in Cúram 8.1.2 for locating rules, descriptions, folders, technical data, and code tables. It also introduces the Business view, a rules-centric hierarchical interface with rules outline, canvas, palettes, and properties/validation panes. The Rules Outline View menu is described, including actions to create folders and rules, delete items, set rule types, move rules, and find references to a selected rule.

children.title: Business View
children.summary: Describes the Business View for users familiar with legislation structure and rule text. It provides a rules-centric interface for creating and maintaining business rules, including a tree/hierarchical rules outline, rules canvas, business element palettes, and properties/validation panes. Also covers the Rules Outline View and its menu actions: create folders and rules, delete items, set rule type/derive attributes, move rules between folders, and find references to a rule.

children.title: Technical View
children.summary: Describes the Technical tab in Cúram Express Rules for technical rule designers. It shows all classes and attributes for the rule being edited, with a Class Outline View listing top-level rules and folders. Covers menu actions such as create class/attribute, delete, set rule type, and find references. Also explains the diagram canvas features: drag-and-drop of rule elements, moving elements between containers, and a toggle to switch between business and detailed technical views, plus pan and zoom controls.

This section introduces the CER editor and its global menu, including Undo/Redo, Include Rule Sets, Export to PNG ZIP, Save/Save All behavior for different rule-set statuses, validation with error reporting, and text search. It also outlines search criteria categories (rules, descriptions, folders, technical data, code tables) and explains the Business View: a rules-centric tree/canvas interface with palettes and validation panes. The Rules Outline View menu supports creating/deleting folders and rules, setting rule types, moving rules, and finding references.

children.title: Diagram Canvas
children.summary: Explains the Diagram Canvas in the CER editor. It supports drag-and-drop of rule elements from palettes onto the diagram, including moving elements between containers when allowed. It also includes a toggle to switch between business and technical views; the business view is simpler, while the technical view is more detailed for rule designers. The toggle is located above the pan and zoom controls.

children.title: Tools and Template Palettes
Business Palettes
children.summary: This section describes the CER editor’s Business palettes (default and extended) and the rule elements available for designing business logic in diagram form. It lists and briefly explains elements such as Rule, And Rule Group, Or Rule Group, Not, Choose, Compare, When, and Arithmetic, including their Boolean, conditional, comparison, and calculation functions. This node is useful if you need to find specific business rule palette elements or understand what each one does.

children.title: Data Types Palette
children.summary: This section describes the Data Types palette in Cúram Express Rules, which provides rule elements for designing data types in diagram form. It lists the available elements: Boolean (true/false, defaults to true), String, Number, and Date, with each mapped to a corresponding literal constant expression. This node is useful if you need the palette’s rule-element options and their basic behavior.

children.title: Technical Palette
children.summary: This section lists the Technical palette rule elements used to build technical logic in diagram form. It describes graphical representations for create, readall/Search, fixedlist, property, custom XML expression, and existencetimeline, each with brief purpose notes. It also references related palette items such as codetable, rate, frequencypattern, resource message, XML message, and null. Useful for understanding which technical rule elements are available and what each represents before opening the detailed pages.

children.title: Household Units Template
children.summary: This section introduces the Household Units template palette in Cúram Express Rules. It explains that the template provides rule elements for designing Household Units diagrams and lists the available palette items: Household Composition and Household Category. It also points readers to the next section, “Rule Element Reference for Household Units Templates” on page 141, for details on those elements.

children.title: Financial Units Template
children.summary: This section introduces two rule-element palettes used to design unit diagrams: the Financial Units template and the Food Assistance Units template. It lists the available palette elements for each, including Financial Unit, Financial Unit Category, and Financial Unit Member, plus Food Assistance Unit, Single Person Category, Multi Person Category, Meal Group Members, Relatives, Discard, Member for household unit, Optional Members, and Exceptions. Most entries point to detailed rule element references on later pages.

children.title: Food Assistance Units Template
children.summary: Describes the Food Assistance Units template palette in Cúram 8.1.2. It explains that the template provides rule elements for designing food assistance units in diagram form and lists the available palette items: Food Assistance Unit, Food Assistance Single Person Category, Food Assistance Multi Person Category, Meal Group Members, Relatives, Discard, Member for household unit, Optional Members, and Exceptions. Most items point to the detailed rule element reference on page 142.

children.title: Decision Table Template
children.summary: Explains the Decision Table template in Cúram Express Rules Reference. Covers the Decision Table palette element and notes it provides a graphical decision table representation, with a pointer to the Rule Element Reference. Also summarizes rule element pop-up menu actions such as cut, copy, delete, collapse/expand, and technical-view timeline options. Introduces rule element properties, including collapsible property and validation panels, and mentions Business and Technical tabs plus general, rule class, and attribute properties.

This section explains the CER editor’s rule palettes and template palettes. It summarizes available elements in Business (default/extended), Data Types, and Technical palettes, including key rule types like Rule, Choose, Compare, Boolean/String/Number/Date, Create, Search, Property, and Null. It also introduces template palettes for Household Units, Financial Units, Food Assistance Units, and the Decision Table, listing their main elements and noting that many point to later detailed references. Useful for finding which rule elements exist and what each palette supports.

children.title: Rule Element Pop-up Menus
children.summary: This section explains the Rule Element pop-up menu in the CER Editor. It covers the general actions available for all rule elements: cut, copy, delete, collapse, expand, and technical-view timeline options (make/remove timeline and timeline interval). It also notes that specific rule element pop-up items are documented elsewhere, and introduces Rule Element Properties and collapsible property/validation panels in the Business and Technical tabs.

children.title: Rule Element Properties
children.summary: Covers CER editor rule element properties and related panels. Explains collapsible Business/Technical property and validation panels, and lists general properties for all rule elements: Display Name, Description, Legislation Link, Display Name ID, and Tag. Also introduces rule class properties such as Primary Attribute, Abstract, Extends, Class, SuccessionSet, and ActiveInEditSuccessionSet, with notes on where each appears and when date attributes are required.

children.title: Rule Element Wizards
children.summary: This section explains the Rule Element Wizards used by rule elements such as Rule and Create Rule to link to rules in the current or external rule set. It describes three options: create an empty rule reference as a placeholder, create a new rule by naming it and choosing a result type, or use an existing rule by selecting it from a list or searching another ruleset.

Introduces the CER editor for creating and maintaining rule sets. Covers the global menu: undo/redo, include rule sets, export diagrams to PNG ZIP, save/save all behavior by rule-set status, validation with error reporting, and text search categories. Explains Business and Technical views, the rules/class outline menus, diagram canvas drag-and-drop and business/technical toggle, pan/zoom controls, palettes and templates, rule element pop-up actions, property/validation panels, general and class/attribute properties, and wizards for creating or linking rule references.

children.title: 1.6 Rule Element Reference for CER Editor Palettes
Palettes
children.summary: This section explains CER editor palettes and how to manage them: undocking/redocking by dragging, and expanding/collapsing to show or hide rule element names and descriptions. It then begins the rule element reference for the default and extended business palettes, focusing on the Rule element. It describes what the Rule element represents and the available actions in Business View vs Technical View, including empty reference, create new rule, and use existing rule, plus switching to Technical View for specific Rule Class or Attribute references.

children.title: Rule Element Reference for the Default and Extended Business Palettes
Rule
children.summary: This section documents the CER Editor “Rule” palette element. It explains that Rule represents a logical rule reference/negation and can be added inside other elements like And/Or Rule Group or Repeating Rule, but not with other palette elements on a Reference element. It covers Business vs Technical View behaviors for creating or selecting rules, including nested/current list scenarios, and lists the key properties (Class, Attribute, Single Item, no/multiple items behavior) plus pop-up actions like Wrap in OR/AND and Edit Rule.

children.title: And Rule Group
children.summary: Describes rule editor palette elements for Boolean logic. The And Rule Group represents an “all” expression, returning true only if all list values are true; it typically takes a fixed list or other Boolean-returning elements and can be nested in And, Or, or When. It also lists pop-up actions: Wrap in AND, Wrap in OR, and Change to Or. The section also briefly introduces Or Rule Group (“any” expression) and Not (negates a Boolean) with related nesting behavior.

children.title: Or Rule Group
children.summary: This section explains the Or Rule Group element in Cúram rules editors. It describes Or as the graphical form of the “any” Boolean expression, taking Boolean values added to its empty member for evaluation. It also notes where Or Rule Group can be used in other palette elements and lists its pop-up menu actions, including wrapping in Or/And groups and changing the element to And. The section sits alongside And Rule Group and Not, with related menu behavior.

children.title: Not
children.summary: Describes the Not rule element in Cúram 8.1.2. It represents logical negation of a Boolean value, can take Boolean-returning palette elements such as Reference, Or, or And, and can itself be added inside And Rule Group, Or Rule Group, or Repeating Rule. The section begins a table of pop-up menu items for this element, but the excerpt does not include the entries.

children.title: Choose
children.summary: Explains the Choose rule element in Cúram Express Rules: a graphical “choose” expression that returns a value when a condition is met. The edit wizard supports multiple data types, including String, Number, Boolean, Date/Datetime, Codetable, Message, timeline, Rule Class, and Java Class. Describes related child elements Only When, Otherwise, and When, plus how Choose can be wrapped in AND/OR groups or added to Compare. Also notes Compare as a related element for left/right value comparison.

children.title: Compare
children.summary: Explains the Compare rule element in Cúram Express Rules: it graphically represents a compare expression, comparing left- and right-side values and creating empty left/right arguments when added. Notes where Compare can be inserted (e.g., When, And Rule Group, Repeating Rule) and its pop-up menu actions: Wrap in OR and Wrap in AND. The surrounding excerpt also briefly describes Choose and When elements and their related menu options.

children.title: When
children.summary: This section defines the When element in Cúram Express Rules. It explains that When is part of a Choose element, containing a test condition and a value returned if the condition passes. It also notes that other palette elements can be inserted into the empty condition (for example, Code or Any) and the empty value (for example, Number or Reference). This node is useful for understanding conditional branching within Choose expressions.

children.title: Arithmetic
children.summary: This section describes Arithmetic, Min, and Max palette elements. Arithmetic evaluates an arithmetic expression on left and right numbers and can optionally round to a chosen number of decimal places, with rounding modes like ceiling, floor, half up, and up. Min returns the smallest value in a comparable fixed list, or null if empty. Max returns the largest value in a similar fixed list, or null if empty. It also notes where these elements can be added and includes pop-up menu options to convert Min to Max and vice versa.

children.title: MIN
children.summary: The MIN section describes the Min element in Cúram 8.1.2 arithmetic expressions. Min returns the smallest value in a fixed list of comparable objects, or null if the list is empty.
