It can contain elements such as Number or Rule Reference and can be used within When or Repeating Rule. The section also notes the Min element’s pop-up menu action: Change To Max, converting it to the Max element.

children.title: MAX
children.summary: MAX describes the Max arithmetic palette element in Cúram 8.1.2. It represents the `max` expression, returning the largest value in a list or null if the list is empty. The fixed list can contain any comparable object, with child palette elements such as Number or Rule Reference added to the empty member. Max can also be used inside other elements like When or Repeating Rule. The pop-up menu includes “Change To Min” to convert it to the Min element.

children.title: SUM
children.summary: This section explains the SUM element in Cúram Express Rules: it graphically represents a sum expression and calculates the numerical total of a list of Number values. It describes how SUM uses a fixed list of numeric objects and can be combined with other palette elements such as Number, Rule Reference, When, and Repeating Rule. The page also briefly transitions into related list-processing elements like Repeating Rule and Filter, including their properties and pop-up menu actions, but SUM itself is the main focus.

children.title: Repeating Rule
children.summary: Explains the Repeating Rule element in Cúram Express Rules. It represents the dynamiclist expression, building a new list by evaluating an expression for each item in an existing list. Describes how it can contain a fixed list and a list-item expression, be nested in rules like And/Or/When, and be combined with elements such as Rule Reference, Sum, or Choose. Lists key properties like Single Item and behavior when no items are found, plus pop-up actions including remove duplicates, concatenate, join inner lists, succeed on any/all, and sum items.

children.title: Filter
children.summary: This section describes the Filter element in Cúram Express Rules. It represents the `filter` expression and builds a new list from an existing list by keeping only items that satisfy a filter condition. It explains how to add palette elements to the Filter’s empty list and list-item expression, and notes that Filter can be embedded in elements such as And, Or, or When. It also introduces that Filter has specific properties listed in the accompanying table.

children.title: Size
children.summary: This section describes the Size element in the Cúram rule/palette editor. It explains that Size represents a property expression, is named “size,” and obtains a property of a Java object. It can contain other palette elements such as Rule Reference or Repeating Rule, and can itself be added to elements like When or Repeating Rule. A small properties table lists the “Value” property (the name of the property element). The excerpt also briefly begins the next topic, Legislation Change.

children.title: Otherwise
children.summary: This section documents the Otherwise element in Cúram 8.1.2. It is part of the Choose element and holds the value returned when no prior choice matches. It can contain child palette elements such as Number or Rule Reference in its empty value. The page also continues adjacent reference content for Filter and Size elements, but the main focus is the Otherwise element’s role and allowed child content.

children.title: Legislation Change
children.summary: Defines the Legislation Change palette element, which represents the `legislationchange` construct and can contain multiple Era elements. The wizard supports ten return data types: String, Number, Boolean, Date, Datetime, List, Message, Codetable, Ruleclass, and Javaclass. The selected data type is used to define the value returned by an Era.

children.title: Era
children.summary: Describes the Era element in Legislation Change rules: it contains an empty from date entry and a return value for the Legislation Change element. Notes that Date or Rule Reference can be added to the date entry, and Choose or Reference can be added to the value. This section also transitions into Data Types palette definitions, introducing Boolean and String elements, their meanings, default state/properties, and basic pop-up/menu behaviors.

Covers CER Editor default/extended business palette elements from Rule through Legislation Change. Key topics include Rule references and nested/current-list scenarios, Boolean groups (And/Or/Not), Choose/When/Otherwise branching, Compare, Arithmetic/Min/Max/SUM, Repeating Rule, Filter, Size/property access, and Legislation Change/Era. Each entry notes where it can be nested, supported child elements, data types, properties, and pop-up actions like wrap/change/edit, remove duplicates, join/concatenate, and convert Min/Max. Useful as a reference for element behavior and menu options.

children.title: Rule Element Reference for Data Types Palette
Boolean
children.summary: This section describes the Boolean data type rule element in Cúram Express Rules. It represents true/false expressions, defaults to true, and can be added to And Rule Group, Or Rule Group, or Repeating Rule elements, but no elements can be added to it. It also lists its pop-up menu actions: Change To False and Change To True. The page also begins the next topic, String, for context.

children.title: String
children.summary: Defines the String data type rule element in Cúram Express Rules. The String element represents a literal string constant, can be added to elements such as When or Repeating Rule, and cannot have child elements. Its main property is Value, the string content shown in the Business Tab. The page also continues the nearby rule-element reference material, including brief context on Boolean and Era elements and their pop-up menu/property behaviors.

children.title: Number
children.summary: This section describes palette elements for constant values: Number, Date, and Code Table. It explains each element’s purpose, where it can be added in the rule/expressions palette, and that no elements can be nested inside them. It also lists their business-tab properties: Number value; Date value and optional Zero Date using Cúram’s zero date; and Code Table name plus selectable codetable value.

children.title: Date
children.summary: This section documents the Date palette element in Cúram 8.1.2. It explains that Date represents a literal Date constant, can be added to other elements like PeriodLength or Era, and has no child elements. It lists its properties: Value (defaulting to today’s date) and Zero Date (to use the Cúram Zero Date). The excerpt also briefly continues adjacent documentation for Number and Code Table elements.

children.title: Code Table
children.summary: This section describes the Code Table palette element in Cúram 8.1.2, which represents a literal code constant from a Cúram code table. It can be added to other elements such as When, but nothing can be nested inside it. The section lists its properties: Codetable Name, which can be left blank to search all tables or used as a prefix filter, and Codetable Value, a dropdown of items from the selected table. Useful for understanding code-table based business expressions.

children.title: Rate
children.summary: This section documents palette elements for rules expressions: Rate, Frequency Pattern, and Resource Message. Rate represents a literal rate from a Cúram rate table and cannot contain child elements; its properties are table name, row, and column. Frequency Pattern represents a literal FrequencyPattern constant, can be added to elements like When or Create, and has a Pattern property plus an edit pop-up action. Resource Message creates a localizable message from a resource property, can be added to other elements, and uses Key and Resource Bundle properties.

children.title: Frequency Pattern
children.summary: This section describes the Frequency Pattern element in Cúram Express Rules. It represents a literal FrequencyPattern constant and can be added to elements like When or Create, but cannot contain other elements. The only listed property is Pattern, shown in the Business Tab. It also documents its pop-up menu, including Edit Frequency Pattern for modifying the selected pattern.

children.title: Resource Message
children.summary: This section explains the Resource Message palette element in Cúram Express Rules. It represents the `ResourceMessage` expression, which creates a localizable message from a property resource. It can be added to other elements such as When or Create, but nothing can be added to it. The section lists its properties: Key (a String used to retrieve a value from a Resource Bundle) and Resource Bundle (the bundle name), both visible in the Business Tab.

children.title: Xml Message
children.summary: The Xml Message element represents the `XmlMessage` expression and creates a message from free-form XML content. It can be added to other palette elements such as When or Create, but no elements can be nested inside it. Its only listed property is Value, which holds the XML resource content, and its pop-up menu includes Edit Message to enter the message content in a dialog.

children.title: Null
children.summary: Defines the Null rule element in Cúram Technical Logic: it graphically represents the `null` value and is used in constructs such as Choose/When and Compare to compare values against null. The excerpt also includes nearby documentation for Xml Message and Create elements, plus pop-up menu/property tables. Open this node if you need null-comparison behavior or related Technical Logic palette element references.

Cúram Express Rules reference for Data Types palette elements. Covers Boolean, String, Number, Date, Code Table, Rate, Frequency Pattern, and Resource Message. For each, it states what constant/expression it represents, where it can be added, that it cannot contain child elements, and its business-tab properties. Also includes Boolean’s Change To True/False pop-up actions and Frequency Pattern’s Edit Frequency Pattern action. Open this node for literal value element behavior and property details.

children.title: Rule Element Reference for Technical Logic Palette
Create
children.summary: Create defines the Technical Logic palette’s create expression, used to instantiate a new rule class in session memory. It supports two parameter types: standard and mandatory. Other palette elements such as Rule Reference, Repeating Rule, and Choose can be added as parameters to Create, and Create itself can be added to elements like Repeating Rule or When. The section introduces its properties table but the excerpt does not show the property names.

children.title: Search
children.summary: Describes the Search element in Cúram Express Rules. Search is a graphical equivalent of the readall expression, retrieving all instances of a rule class created by client code, or a single item if singleitem is selected. It can be added to elements like Filter or Create, but no elements can be added to Search. The section lists Search properties: Class, Rule Set, Single Item, and behaviors for no items or multiple items found, plus the Edit Search pop-up menu item.

children.title: Fixed List
children.summary: Describes the Fixed List and Property rule elements in Cúram 8.1.2. Fixed List represents a fixedlist expression, creating a list of design-time items, supports nine data types (String, Number, Boolean, Date, Date time, Codetable Entry, Rule Class, Java Class, Message), and can be wrapped/combined with other rule elements depending on type. Property represents a property expression for a Java object, can take Rule Reference or Repeating Rule children, and has menu actions like wrap in OR/AND and add/remove arguments.

children.title: Property
children.summary: The Property element represents the `property` expression and is used to obtain a property from a Java object. It can contain other palette elements such as Rule Reference or Repeating Rule, and can itself be added to elements like When or Repeating Rule. Its main property is Value, which names the property element. The pop-up menu supports wrapping in OR/AND, adding a new argument, and removing an argument.

children.title: Custom Expression
children.summary: Custom Expression describes a CER element for representing any user-defined valid XML node graphically. It can be added anywhere in CER, but its node name must not conflict with existing CER language node names. The section also lists its popup menu item, “Edit Custom Expression,” which opens the editor for the expression.

children.title: Existence Timeline
children.summary: Explains the CER “Existence Timeline” element. It graphically represents the `existence-timeline` expression, can take Date or Rule Reference inputs for FromDate, ToDate, preExistenceValue, postExistenceValue, and ExistenceValue, and can be nested inside other palette elements such as Repeating Rule. Also describes its property item “Data Type,” including options to mark it as Timeline or list in the Business tab.

children.title: Timeline
children.summary: This section explains the Timeline and Interval UI elements for an existence timeline rule. It describes the Timeline’s purpose as a graphical representation of a Timeline expression, how to set an initial value, add intervals or other palette elements, and switch its data type between Timeline and list. It also covers Interval as a graphical representation of an Interval expression, how it can be used for FromDate/ToDate, its child elements, and its popup menu option to edit the interval wizard.

children.title: Interval
children.summary: This section describes the Interval element in Cúram existence timelines. It explains that Interval graphically represents an “Interval” expression, can only be added to a Timeline’s Intervals element, and may use Date or Reference elements for FromDate and ToDate. It also notes that an Interval has start and value child elements, can be configured with the interval wizard, and has a single pop-up action: Edit Interval.

children.title: CombineSuccessionSet
children.summary: This section describes the CombineSuccessionSet graphical rule element and its pop-up menu option for editing the referenced rule class and rule set. It also introduces the Call element, which represents the `call` expression for invoking a static Java method, including how to add arguments and nested palette elements. Key Call properties are Class, Method Name, and Date Type (including Timeline/list options), plus pop-up actions to wrap the call in AND/OR groups and manage arguments.

children.title: Call
children.summary: This section documents the Call palette element in Cúram Express Rules. It explains that Call represents a call expression used to invoke a static Java method for complex calculations, and can take arguments built from other palette elements such as Date, Period Length, or Rule Reference. It lists key properties: Class, Method Name, and Date Type, including options for Timeline and list return types. It also covers pop-up menu actions like wrapping in AND/OR, adding a new argument, and removing an argument.

children.title: Period Length
children.summary: Defines the Period Length CER element, which graphically represents the `periodlength` expression and calculates the time between two dates. It can accept palette elements like Date, Call, Rule Reference, and can be added to elements such as Create. Key properties are Unit (days, weeks, months, years) and EndDateInclusion (inclusive/exclusive). The excerpt also notes CER/Dependency Manager auto-recalculate stored values when dependencies change, but static method changes require creating a new method and updating rule sets.

children.title: ALL
children.summary: Explains CER/Dependency Manager recalculation behavior: changing a static method implementation won’t automatically trigger recalculation of stored attribute values, so new implementations should be added as new static methods and rule sets updated to reference them. Also describes the Period Length element, which represents a periodlength expression and computes time between dates, with unit and end-date inclusion properties. The ALL element represents an all expression returning a Boolean over a list of Boolean values and supports nesting with other palette elements and pop-up menu actions like wrap/change to OR/AND.

children.title: ANY
children.summary: This section documents rule palette elements in Cúram Express Rules: ANY, This, Sort, and Shared Rule Reference. It explains that ANY evaluates a list of Boolean values and returns true if any are true, supports wrapping/changing to AND/OR groups, and can be combined with Repeating Rule or Fixed List. It also describes This as a reference to the current Rule Object, Sort as a list-sorting expression with ascending/descending items, and Shared Rule Reference as a wizard-based reference to primary-attribute rule classes.

children.title: This
children.summary: This section documents Cúram Express Rules palette elements: ANY, This, Sort, and Shared Rule Reference. It explains what each expression does, how it behaves in the diagram, and relevant context-menu/property options. ANY evaluates whether any Boolean in a list is true; This refers to the current Rule Object and can be added to elements like When or Any; Sort orders a list with ascending/descending items; Shared Rule Reference points to a rule class with a primary attribute and can be edited via a wizard.

children.title: Sort
children.summary: Sort describes the graphical “sort” expression in Cúram Express Rules. It takes a list child element and sorts it. The only listed pop-up menu actions are to add a new ascending sort item or a new descending sort item. This section is brief and mainly documents how to configure sort items in the rule diagram.

children.title: Shared Rule Reference
children.summary: Explains the Shared Rule Reference element in Cúram Express Rules. A Shared Rule Reference is a normal Reference containing a Create element and is used to reference rule classes marked with a Primary attribute in the current rule set. It can be edited via the “Edit Shared Rule Reference” diagram option, and a Shared Rule Wizard helps create shared rules and choose eligible classes. Also notes its main property: Class, shown in the Business tab.

children.title: CONCAT
children.summary: CONCAT describes the Concat element, which represents the `concat` expression and builds a localizable message by concatenating a list of values. It uses a fixed list of String objects and can accept palette elements like String or Rule Reference; it can also be nested inside elements such as When or Repeating Rule. The section also notes its property: Data Type, which must match the attribute type and can be set as Timeline or list. It briefly begins the next topic, JoinLists.

children.title: Join Lists
children.summary: Explains the JoinLists palette element in Cúram rule modeling. It represents the `joinlists` expression and builds a new list by combining existing lists. The section notes you can add elements like Rule Reference or Choose into its empty member, and the JoinLists element itself can be placed inside And Rule Group, Or Rule Group, or When elements.

This section is a reference for Technical Logic palette elements in Cúram Express Rules, covering Create, Search, Fixed List, Property, Custom Expression, Existence Timeline, Timeline, Interval, CombineSuccessionSet, Call, Period Length, ALL, ANY, This, Sort, Shared Rule Reference, Concat, and JoinLists. It summarizes each element’s expression, where it can be used/embedded, key properties, and context-menu actions. It also notes dependency recalculation behavior for stored values when property or static methods change.

children.title: Rule Element Reference for Household Units Templates
children.summary: This section introduces rule elements for Household Units templates: Household Composition and Household Category. It briefly defines Household Composition as representing a household composition used in Cúram Income Support rules, and Household Category as a new household category within CGIS rules. It also lists Household Category pop-up menu actions for managing mandatory and optional members. The excerpt additionally shows Join Lists property and menu options such as single-item behavior, no/multiple-item handling, duplicate removal, concatenation, and joining inner lists.

children.title: Rule Element Reference for Financial Units Templates
children.summary: This section lists available rule elements for Financial Units and Food Assistance Units templates in Cúram 8.1.2. It describes Financial Unit, Financial Unit Category, and Financial Unit Member for financial unit modeling, then covers Food Assistance Unit, Single Person Category, Multi Person Category, and Meal Group Members. It also notes that Food Assistance Unit diagrams require prior configuration and that the Multi Person Category element has specific pop-up actions for removing meal groups, relatives, discard, head of household members, and optional members.

children.title: Rule Element Reference for Food Assistance Units Templates
Discard
children.summary: This section defines the “Discard” template for new food assistance discard within Income Support rules, alongside related templates like Relatives, Member for Household Unit, Optional Members, and Exceptions. It then introduces the Rule Element Reference for Decision Table Templates, describing decision tables in the rule editor, including creation options (number of rows up to 99, result type, rule class) and properties such as class, attribute, single item, and behavior when no items are found.

Describes Food Assistance Units templates in Income Support rules. It lists available rule elements: Food Assistance Unit, Food Assistance Single Person Category, Food Assistance Multi Person Category, and Meal Group Members. Food Assistance Unit requires a configured food assistance diagram before full editing or use of certain wizards. The Multi Person Category includes pop-up actions to remove meal groups, relatives, discard, head of household members, and optional members.

children.title: Rule Element Reference for Decision Table Templates
children.summary: Explains the Decision Table rule element and its wizard/options when dragged onto a rule or attribute. Covers required setup fields: number of rows (up to 99), result type matching the containing rule/attribute, and rule class selection. Also lists key properties: Class, Attribute, Single Item, and behavior when no items are found (error or null when Single Item is checked). Useful for understanding Decision Table configuration and available UI properties.

This section is a reference for CER editor palettes and rule elements, including palette docking/expansion controls. It details Business/Technical View behavior and properties/menu actions for default and extended business elements (Rule, Boolean groups, Choose/When, Compare, Arithmetic, Min/Max, etc.), plus later palette families: Data Types, Technical Logic, Household/Financial/Food Assistance templates, and Decision Table templates. Useful as a lookup for element meanings, nesting rules, properties, and pop-up actions like wrap, edit, change, and list handling.

children.title: 1.7 CER Best Practices
Creating a Rule Attribute Description
children.summary: Explains why and how to create a rule attribute description for CER rule classes. The root rule class has a default description, but rule sets should override it because the description is shown in RuleDoc and via toString in Java IDEs. Descriptions are localizable and can be as simple or complex as needed. The CER validator warns if a rule class lacks a description attribute. Includes XML and Java examples showing custom descriptions for Person and Income, and the default description for an undeclared Benefit class.

children.title: Getting a Rule Set Working Quickly
children.summary: Explains shortcuts for getting a CER rule set runnable quickly: create empty rule classes, hard-code derivations, use plain single-locale strings for messages, and use String values instead of code table values, with the intent to replace them later. Emphasizes that these shortcuts only defer work, not eliminate it, and that tests should be created early. Also warns against skipping description rule attributes because they help debugging, and gives naming guidance for rule elements and rule sets (meaning-based names; rule set names use letters/numbers/underscores and start with an uppercase letter).

children.title: Naming Rule Elements
children.summary: This section advises naming CER rule attributes by their business meaning and expected result, not by implementation details. It also states rule set names may contain unaccented letters, numbers, and underscores, and should begin with an uppercase letter. It appears alongside guidance on getting rule sets working quickly, including shortcuts for empty rule classes, hard-coded derivations, plain strings, and deferred localization, while stressing that tests should still be created early.

children.title: Using the Reference Expression
children.summary: Guidelines for using reference expressions in CER rules: balance the number of rule attributes and references, avoiding both overly deep/complex expressions and unnecessary encapsulation. Create rule attributes for meaningful shared calculations or strong concepts that don’t map neatly to one attribute; inline logic if a referenced attribute is hard to name or only used once. The section also notes that the CER editor supports reference scenarios and recommends RuleDoc for understanding larger, more complex rule sets.

children.title: Using RuleDoc
children.summary: This section covers naming rules for CER rule elements: class names use unaccented letters, numbers, and underscores and should start with an uppercase letter; attribute names follow the same pattern but start with a lowercase letter, with camelCase recommended. It also explains that rule element names are not localizable and labels should use the Label annotation. The section gives guidance on using reference expressions effectively and recommends RuleDoc for understanding larger, more complex rule sets.

children.title: Normalizing Common Rules
children.summary: Guidance on consolidating similar CER rules. Explains two mechanisms: inheritance for true “is-a” relationships and containment for “has-a” relationships, with examples and the note that CER does not support multiple inheritance. Also advises using common rule sets to share artefacts across rule sets. Covers removing unused rules and attributes, including CER’s unused-attribute report and editor delete tools, with a warning that some apparently unused top-level attributes may still be needed by client code or tests.

children.title: Removing Unused Rules
children.summary: Explains how to remove unused CER rules after centralizing common logic. Notes that some rule attributes may no longer be referenced and can be deleted, and CER can report attributes not used by other calculations. The CER editor can delete rule classes and attributes, and there is a report for unused attributes. Warns that top-level attributes may be used only by client code or tests, so “unused” results should be checked carefully before removal.

children.title: Changing the Order of Declarations
children.summary: This section explains that declaration order usually does not affect rule behavior: rule classes and calculated attributes can be reordered freely. The exception is initialized attributes in a rule class, whose values must be specified in the defined order when creating rule objects; changing them requires updating all creation sites. It also notes that boolean conditions in all/any expressions can be reordered for performance because evaluation stops early, and gives guidance on deciding whether rule objects are created by application code or by rules.

children.title: Creating Rule Objects
children.summary: This section explains how declaration order usually does not affect rule behavior, including rule classes, calculated attributes, and Boolean conditions in all/any expressions. It notes that initialized attributes are different: their values must be supplied in the exact order defined, so reordering them requires updating all creation code and rules. It also advises ordering Boolean conditions for faster short-circuit evaluation. Finally, it highlights that rule object creation strategy should be planned early, especially deciding whether objects are created by application code or by rules.

children.title: Passing Rule Objects Instead of IDs
children.summary: Use rule objects, not raw IDs, when initializing created internal rule objects via create expressions. Passing a rule object improves type safety, helps prevent accidental use of the wrong external identifier, and lets the CER rule set validator catch mismatches at design time instead of causing runtime failures. The section also introduces developing static methods: when CER expressions are insufficient, the call expression can invoke a static method on a custom Java class.

children.title: Developing Static Methods
children.summary: Explains how to implement CER static methods when expressions aren’t enough. Shows a rule set calling a Java static method via the `call` element, first with strongly typed generated rule classes for development, then a production-ready weakly typed version using `RuleObject`. Notes that changing an existing static method won’t trigger recalculation of stored values; instead create a new method and repoint rule sets. Static methods must be deterministic, use only passed data, and have no side effects.

children.title: Avoiding Common Pitfalls in Tests
children.summary: This section lists common CER JUnit test pitfalls: use CREOLETestHelper.assertEquals instead of JUnit assertEquals for numeric values; always call .getValue() on attribute calculators; specify every required value before calculation; do not specify the same value twice under RecalculationsProhibited; and pass the correct value type to specifyValue. It also advises creating all rule objects in a session before any getValue-based calculations begin, since later creations can invalidate prior results.

CER best practices cover: defining meaningful, localizable description attributes for rule classes (to improve RuleDoc, toString, and debugging); quick-start shortcuts such as empty classes, hard-coded derivations, plain strings, and String codes while still writing tests early; naming rules by business meaning and using valid class/attribute naming conventions; using reference expressions and RuleDoc judiciously; and testing pitfalls, including use of CREOLETestHelper.assertEquals, .getValue(), specifying all required values, avoiding duplicate specifications, correct value types, and creating all session rule objects before calculations.

children.title: 1.8 CER XML Dictionary
Rule Set
children.summary: Explains rule-set testing constraints when calculations have already started: adding new rule objects afterward can invalidate prior readall calculations and trigger RecalculationsProhibited runtime errors, so all rule objects should be created before any calculation. Also introduces the CER XML Dictionary definition of a rule set: it has a name, contains rule classes and include statements, may include annotations, and is constrained by the dynamically built RuleSet.xsd schema.

children.title: Include Statement
children.summary: Explains the Include statement in Cúram Express Rules: it lets a rule set be split into smaller reusable pieces for parallel development and reuse. Includes can pull in either a single Class or an entire Ruleset, and nested include statements are processed recursively. Also introduces supported include types, starting with RelativePath, which includes an XML file via a path relative to the containing file for file-based development.

children.title: Rule Class
children.summary: Explains the Rule Class element in Creole rules. A rule class defines the behavior of its instances, must have a unique name within the rule set, and can be abstract. It may contain an Initialization block for attributes that must be supplied when instances are created, in defined order, plus calculated attributes defined directly in the class. The section warns not to reorder initialized attributes because creation code may depend on that order, while calculated attributes can be reordered safely.

children.title: Attribute
children.summary: Defines the CER rule class Attribute element. An attribute must have a unique name within the rule class, plus a type and a derivation. The type sets the value type; the derivation contains a single CER expression that computes the value. Notes that special marker expressions can change rule-attribute semantics. The section also begins an overview of CER expressions, including boolean logic, value comparison, and constants.

children.title: Expressions
Boolean Logic
children.summary: This section lists CER expressions grouped by function. Under Boolean Logic, it includes true, false, all, any, and not, all of which evaluate to boolean values. It also notes that some expressions appear in multiple logical groups. The surrounding page also introduces attribute definitions, where each attribute has a unique name, a type, and a derivation defined by a single CER expression, with special marker expressions able to alter attribute semantics.

children.title: Value Comparison
children.summary: This section introduces CER expressions and groups them by function. The “Value Comparison” group lists expressions for comparing values: equals, compare, and sort. It appears alongside related expression categories such as Boolean Logic and Constants, and sits within the broader discussion of rule attributes and derivations.

children.title: Constants
children.summary: This section lists CER literal constant expressions used in rule derivations: true, false, null, String, Number, Date, Code, and FrequencyPattern. It sits within the broader expressions reference and helps identify expressions that provide fixed values rather than computed results.

children.title: Conditional Logic
children.summary: Overview of conditional-logic-related expression types in Cúram Express Rules. It lists categories and key functions: choose; list aggregations (all, any, sum, min, max, concat, singleitem); list transformations (dynamiclist, fixedlist, filter, joinlists, removeduplicates, sort, sublists); localizable message expressions (concat, ResourceMessage, XmlMessage); numerical calculations (Number, arithmetic, periodlength, sum, max, min); and references via reference. Also points to Java List operations elsewhere.

children.title: List Aggregations
children.summary: Lists the aggregation expressions that reduce a list of values to a single derived value: all, any, sum, min, max, concat, and singleitem. This section is a navigation entry for list aggregation operations in Cúram Express Rules; for list interface operations, it points to Useful List Operations on page 246.

children.title: List Transformations
children.summary: This section lists list-transformation expressions in Cúram Express Rules, used to create new lists from existing ones. It includes dynamiclist, fixedlist, filter, joinlists, removeduplicates, sort, and sublists, with page references for each. It sits within a broader catalog of expression categories (conditional logic, aggregations, messages, numerical calculations, references) and points readers to Java List operations for additional list functionality.

children.title: Localizable Messages
children.summary: This section lists expressions for creating end-user locale-specific messages in Cúram Express Rules. It covers message-related expressions such as concat, ResourceMessage, and XmlMessage, with page references for each. Useful if you need to build or localize user-facing text rather than perform logic or calculations.

children.title: Numerical Calculations
children.summary: Lists expressions for numerical calculations in Cúram Express Rules. It covers numeric conversion and operations via Number, arithmetic, periodlength, sum, max, and min, with links to the relevant pages for each expression. Use this section if you need rules for computing numeric values, aggregating numbers, or comparing numeric extrema.

children.title: References
children.summary: Reference index for Cúram Express Rules expressions. Groups expressions by purpose: conditional logic, list aggregations and transformations, localizable messages, numerical calculations, and references to other items. Includes links to key expressions such as choose, all/any/sum/min/max/concat/singleitem, dynamiclist/fixedlist/filter/joinlists/removeduplicates/sort/sublists, ResourceMessage/XmlMessage, Number/arithmetic/periodlength, and reference. Also points to Java List operations for additional list behavior.

children.title: Creation
children.summary: The Creation section defines the CER create expression, which creates a new rule object. On this page, it also serves as an index entry among related expression categories, including retrieval, Java callouts, markers, timelines, and product delivery eligibility/entitlement. This node is useful if you need the specific create expression or nearby navigation to related expression types.

children.title: Retrieval
children.summary: This section defines the Retrieval expression category in CER. It explains that the readall expression retrieves rule objects and points to its detailed reference on page 217. The surrounding index also situates Retrieval among other expression groups like Creation, Java Callouts, Markers, Timelines, and Product Delivery Eligibility and Entitlement, helping readers locate related expression types and their pages.

children.title: Java Callouts
children.summary: Lists CER expression categories and the Java callout expressions. Java Callouts are expressions that invoke Java code to perform calculations, with references to `property` and `call`. The surrounding page also points to related expression groups: Creation (`create`), Retrieval (`readall`), Markers (`abstract`, `specified`), Timelines (`Interval`, `Timeline`, `existencetimeline`, `intervalvalue`, `timelineoperation`), and Product Delivery Eligibility/Entitlement (`combineSuccessionSets`, `legislationChange`, `rate`).

children.title: Markers
children.summary: Defines CER marker expressions, which are special non-calculation expressions. It lists the marker types abstract (p.170) and specified (p.229), alongside nearby expression categories such as Creation, Retrieval, Java Callouts, Timelines, and Product Delivery Eligibility/Entitlement. Useful as a navigation point if you need the available marker expressions rather than calculation logic.

children.title: Timelines
children.summary: Lists CER timeline-processing expressions. Notes that CER timelines are covered in “Handling Data that Changes Over Time” and points to the related expressions Interval, Timeline, existencetimeline, intervalvalue, and timelineoperation. This section is mainly an index of timeline-related expression names with page references, useful if you need timeline semantics or the specific expression definitions.

children.title: Product Delivery Eligibility and Entitlement
children.summary: This section lists CER expressions used for product delivery eligibility and entitlement calculations. It identifies business-specific calculation expressions, including combineSuccessionSets, legislationChange, and rate, with page references for each. Use it to find the expression set relevant to product delivery cases and to locate the detailed descriptions of these eligibility/entitlement functions.

children.title: Full Alphabetical Listing of Expressions
abstract
children.summary: Defines the CER “abstract” marker for rule-class attributes, meaning their derivation must be provided by a concrete subclass or superclass. The validator requires any class with abstract attributes to be marked abstract="true", and such classes cannot be used in create expressions. Includes an XML example showing an abstract Benefit base class with abstract name and isEligible attributes, and a concrete MedicalBenefit subclass supplying their derivations.

children.title: all
children.summary: Defines the Cúram Express Rules `all` expression, which returns true only if every Boolean in a list is true, stopping at the first false and returning true for an empty list. The list usually comes from a `fixedlist` or `dynamiclist`, and item order doesn’t affect correctness but can improve performance. Notes Cúram V6 error-handling behavior: child errors are suppressed if the overall result is already determined, unless immediate exception reporting is enabled. Includes an XML example using `all` to derive `isLoneParent`.

children.title: any
children.summary: Explains the Cúram Express Rules `any` expression, which evaluates a list of Boolean values and returns true if any item is true, otherwise false for an empty list. It stops at the first true value, so ordering can improve performance, especially in fixed lists. Notes that child-expression errors are ignored when they do not affect the overall result, but an error is returned if it prevents determining whether any value is true. Includes examples using `fixedlist` and `dynamiclist` in rule derivations.

children.title: arithmetic
children.summary: Describes the CER arithmetic expression, which calculates on two numbers and optionally rounds the result. Covers supported operations: addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division. Explains that rounding requires both a decimal-place count and a rounding mode (see JavaDoc RoundingMode). Warns that division should usually specify rounding; otherwise runtime errors can occur if an exact result is not possible, and the validator flags such cases. Includes XML examples for each operation and chained arithmetic.

children.title: call
children.summary: The call expression invokes a static Java method for complex calculations. It declares a return type and optional arguments, and the target class must be on the classpath during rule set validation. The Java method’s first parameter must be a Session, followed by parameters matching the rule set. A warning notes invoked code should not mutate rule object attributes or custom mutable data types. An example shows a Java helper computing a person’s favorite color from name and age.

children.title: choose
children.summary: Explains the `choose` expression in Cúram Express Rules. A `choose` can declare a type, an optional test expression, one or more `when` clauses, and an `otherwise` result. Conditions are evaluated top-down and stop at the first match; later conditions are not evaluated. It acts like if/else-if/else and can model decision tables. Includes a warning that reordering conditions is only safe for simple cases, plus an age-category example using nested `when` clauses and `otherwise`.

children.title: Code
children.summary: Explains the CER Code expression, which returns a literal code-table value from a specified application code table. It takes the table name and a single string argument for the code value; generated code constants cannot be used because CER is fully dynamic. Includes an XML example deriving a Person.gender attribute from isMale using the Gender code table and string codes like "MALE" and "FEMALE".

children.title: combineSuccessionSets
children.summary: This section only names `combineSuccessionSets` and points to external documentation in “Developing with Eligibility and Entitlement by Using Cúram Express Rules.” The visible page content mainly covers the related `compare` expression, which compares left and right values using `<`, `<=`, `>`, or `>=` on comparable types such as Number, String, and `curam.util.type.Date`.

children.title: compare
children.summary: The compare expression evaluates a left-side value against a right-side value using one of four comparisons: <, <=, >, or >=. It supports comparable object types such as Number, String, and curam.util.type.Date. Use this section for the syntax and meaning of comparison operations in Cúram Express Rules.

children.title: concat
children.summary: Explains the CER `concat` expression, which builds a localizable message by concatenating a list of values with no added spaces. Notes that it’s suitable for simple text assembly, while `ResourceMessage` should be used for more complex formatting/localizable text. Includes an example `Person.personIdentifier` attribute combining first name, surname, and localized date of birth using a `fixedlist` of mixed `String` and `Date` values.

children.title: create
children.summary: Describes the CER `create` expression, which instantiates a new rule class in session memory and can optionally target a different ruleset. Explains two ways to pass initialization values: an `Initialization` block or `specify` elements. Notes that created rule objects are pooled, so identical create requests share one instance when the class and all initialized/specified attribute values match. Also warns these objects cannot be retrieved during rule execution due to CER ordering. Includes an XML example creating `PhoneNumber` objects in a `Person` list.

children.title: current
children.summary: Explains the `current` expression in Cúram Express Rules: it refers to the item currently being processed in a list. It can be used only inside list-processing expressions such as `filter` listitem expressions, dynamic lists, and `sort` sortorder expressions. The section also notes that `current` can be given an alias matching the list expression’s alias, and aliases are required when multiple `current` expressions are in scope. An XML example shows `current` used in a `filter` to derive adults from a household’s members.

children.title: Date
children.summary: Defines the CER Date expression as a literal `curam.util.type.Date` constant in `yyyy-mm-dd` format, with examples of a null date and a specific date value. Notes that CER intentionally has no current-date function because volatile functions are forbidden. The excerpt also begins the next topic, `dynamiclist`, which creates a new list by applying an expression to each item in an existing list while preserving order.

children.title: dynamiclist
children.summary: Explains the CER dynamiclist expression: it builds a new list by applying a listitemexpression to each item of an existing list, preserving order and one-to-one item count. Use it when the output list size is not known at design time; if fixed, use fixedlist instead. Includes an example rule set showing a Household attribute deriving a Boolean from members’ isDisabled values via dynamiclist within an any expression.

children.title: defaultDescription
children.summary: Explains the `defaultDescription` expression, which provides the inherited default implementation for the `description` attribute in the root rule class. It notes that child rule classes should override `description` with a more meaningful value, or validation will warn if no override exists. The expression is for root-rule-class use only. Includes a short XML example and references handling data types.

children.title: equals
children.summary: Explains the `equals` expression in Cúram Express Rules. It compares two objects for equality, converting numbers to CER’s BigDecimal-based numeric format so leading/trailing zeros are ignored. It handles nulls safely: both null returns true, one null returns false. Examples show true/false cases for identical and different strings, numbers with different zero padding, different types, and null comparisons.

children.title: existencetimeline
children.summary: The existencetimeline expression creates a Timeline of a chosen type from optional inclusive start and end dates. It can define values before the start date, during the existence interval, and after the end date. The section explains its syntax and shows an example using a Person’s dateOfBirth and dateOfDeath to derive timelines such as isAliveTimeline and lifeStatus, with pre-, in-, and post-existence values.

children.title: false
children.summary: Defines the `false` expression in Cúram rules as the Boolean constant value `false`. Includes an XML example showing `false` used directly and inside a `not` expression. The page also begins the next topic, `filter`, describing it as creating a new list from an existing list by keeping items that satisfy a condition, with inputs `list` and `listitemexpression`.

children.title: filter
children.summary: Explains the `filter` expression in Cúram Express Rules. `filter` creates a new list from an existing list by keeping only items that satisfy a `listitemexpression`, while preserving the original order. If no items match, it returns an empty list. The section defines the required parts (`list` and `listitemexpression`) and shows an example filtering household members to produce adult members (`age >= 18`).

children.title: fixedlist
children.summary: Describes the `fixedlist` expression for creating a list from items known at rule-set design time. It specifies `listof` (item type) and `members` (0, 1, or many items), and preserves member order. Notes the CER validator may warn if used in a list-processing operation with only 0 or 1 members, suggesting the list may be unnecessary. Recommends `dynamic list` when the number of items is not known at design time. Includes XML examples for lists of `Pet` and `Boolean`.

children.title: FrequencyPattern
children.summary: Defines the `FrequencyPattern` literal expression in Cúram rules. It creates a constant value of type `curam.util.type.FrequencyPattern`, represented as a 9-digit string whose meaning is documented in the JavaDoc. Includes XML examples showing a null pattern and a weekly-on-Mondays pattern (`100100100`). The page then begins the next topic, `Interval`, which creates a time-valid value from a specified date and is used in Timeline construction.

children.title: Interval
children.summary: Introduces the Interval expression, which creates an interval of a specified type starting from a given date. It notes that Interval is commonly used when constructing a Timeline and refers readers to “Handling Data that Changes Over Time” for more detail.

children.title: intervalvalue
children.summary: `intervalvalue` is an expression that wraps another expression returning a `Timeline`, letting an outer expression work with the Timeline’s individual values instead of the Timeline itself. It “shields” the outer expression from Timeline details. It can only be used nested inside a `timelineoperation` expression. For more detail and examples, the section points to `timelineoperation` and Handling Data that Changes Over Time.

children.title: joinlists
children.summary: `joinlists` creates a new list by concatenating a list of lists. It accepts one argument: a list whose elements are lists. The resulting order preserves each source list’s item order and joins the lists in the order given. The section notes that if duplicates may occur, `removeduplicates` can be applied after `joinlists`. An example shows building a household’s `allPets` by joining each member’s `pets` list via a `dynamiclist`.

children.title: legislationChange
children.summary: Describes the `legislationChange` expression and points readers to Developing with Eligibility and Entitlement by Using Cúram Express Rules for details. Also documents `max`, which returns the largest value in a list or null if the list is empty. The list items must be comparable, such as Number, String, or curam.util.type.Date.

children.title: max
children.summary: Explains the CER max expression. It returns the largest value in a list, or null if the list is empty. Supported comparable list items include Number, String, and curam.util.type.Date. Numbers are converted to CER’s BigDecimal-based numeric format before comparison. Includes XML examples showing max used on a fixed list of strings and on a dynamic list to find the greatest number of spots among dalmatians.

children.title: min
children.summary: Defines the CER `min` expression, which returns the smallest value in a list or `null` if the list is empty. It works on comparable values such as Number, String, and `curam.util.type.Date`; all Numbers are converted to CER’s BigDecimal-based numeric format before comparison. Includes XML examples showing `min` over a fixed list of dates and over a dynamic list of zebra stripe counts.

children.title: not
children.summary: Explains the Cúram rules `not` expression, which negates a Boolean value. Includes an XML example showing `not` used to derive `isLivingInUSA` from `isLivingOutsideUSA`, and `isLivingOutsideUSA` as the negation of `country = "USA"`. Also briefly notes `null` as a null constant value.

children.title: null
children.summary: Cúram Express Rules reference entries for the `not` and `null` expressions. `not` negates a Boolean value, with examples showing nested negation and a country check. `null` represents an empty/absent value and is useful when no value applies. Example rules show a `Person` with an optional `favoritePet` and a derived `favoritePetsName` that returns `null` when no pet exists, otherwise returns the pet’s name using a `choose`/`when` test for null.

children.title: Number
children.summary: Defines the CER `Number` literal constant. A Number is an arbitrarily long decimal using `.` as the decimal separator and no thousands separators. It can also be written as a percentage by suffixing `%`; e.g. `12.345%` is equivalent to `0.12345`, but displays as a percentage. Includes XML examples for positive, negative, decimal, and percentage Number values.

children.title: periodlength
children.summary: Describes the `periodlength` expression in CER, which calculates elapsed time between two dates. It supports units days, weeks, months, and years, and requires specifying whether the end date is inclusive or exclusive; the start date is always inclusive. Results are rounded down to the nearest integer. Includes examples showing same-day and year-spanning calculations with inclusive/exclusive end-date behavior.

children.title: property
children.summary: Explains the CER `property` expression, which calls a safe Java method on an object with optional arguments. It supports leveraging Java methods without duplicating them in CER, but only methods on class safe lists may be used; unsafe or missing methods cause validation errors. Shows safe-list files for `Timeline`, `Object`, `Number`, and `List`, and examples using `valueOn`, `isEmpty`, `size`, and `subList` in rule definitions for `Person` attributes.

children.title: rate
children.summary: The “rate” section is just a pointer to other Cúram Express Rules documentation (“Developing with Eligibility and Entitlement by Using Cúram Express Rules”) for the rate expression. It appears alongside guidance on stored calculated attribute values and a warning that changing property method implementations won’t trigger automatic recalculation; instead, create a new property method and republish rule sets.

children.title: readall
children.summary: Explains the readall expression in CER. It retrieves all external rule object instances of a rule class (not internal ones), can optionally target another rule set via ruleset, and supports a match element to filter by an attribute value at retrieval time. Notes that in Cúram V6+ readall/match is generally more performant than wrapping readall in filter, especially with DatabaseDataStorage and recalculation. Recommends using filter for additional criteria and singleitem when only one instance is expected. Includes XML examples.

children.title: reference
children.summary: Explains the CER “reference” expression in Cúram Express Rules: it retrieves an attribute value from a rule object, optionally using a child expression to choose the object, otherwise defaulting to the containing rule object. Emphasizes that references make rules reusable and readable, and can be used wherever an expression is allowed. Notes that the validator errors if the referenced attribute’s type does not match the required type. Includes XML examples showing simple same-object references and references within classes like Person, Pet, and Household.

children.title: removeduplicates
children.summary: The removeduplicates expression returns a new list with duplicate items removed, preserving the original order and keeping only the first occurrence of each repeated item. The section includes an example using a Person class where uniqueRelatives is derived from allRelatives via removeduplicates, illustrating how duplicate relatives are collapsed to a single entry.

children.title: ResourceMessage
children.summary: ResourceMessage creates a localizable message from a property resource bundle. It supports optional MessageFormat-style placeholders for formatted arguments, and single quotes in property text must be doubled. The section points to Localizing CER Rules and notes XmlMessage as an alternative when outputting XML/HTML without complex token formatting or runtime message text changes. An example RuleSet shows ResourceMessage used to build localized greeting messages, including parameterized messages.

children.title: singleitem
children.summary: Explains the `singleitem` expression, which extracts one item from a list. It is intended for lists expected to contain exactly one result, such as after filtering. The section defines behavior for empty lists (`error` or `returnNull`) and multi-item lists (`error`, `returnNull`, `returnFirst`, `returnLast`). Includes XML examples showing use for the first child in a sorted list and for retrieving a required singleton record from external storage.

children.title: sort
children.summary: Describes the `sort` expression in Cúram Express Rules. It creates a new list by sorting an existing list without changing the source list. The `sortorder` contains one or more `sortitem`s, ordered by significance; each item can sort ascending or descending and typically uses `current` to reference the list item. Equal items retain their original relative order. Includes an example sorting household members by age descending, then first name ascending, like SQL `ORDER BY`.

children.title: specified
children.summary: Describes the `specified` expression in Cúram Express Rules as a marker for attributes whose values are provided externally rather than calculated by rules. These values may come from a database, test code, or other outside source, and are often used as inputs for deriving other attributes. Includes an XML example of a `Person` class where `name` and `dateOfBirth` are marked with `<specified/>`.

children.title: String
children.summary: Defines the `String` literal constant type in Cúram rules, with examples of an empty string and `"Hello, World!"` using `<String value="..."/>` inside rule class attributes. Note: the page also begins the next topic, `sublists`, which describes generating all sublists of a list (including empty and original), totaling `2^n` sublists for a list of `n` elements.

children.title: sublists
children.summary: The section defines the `sublists` expression in Cúram Express Rules. It returns all possible sublists of a list as a list of lists, preserving the original item order. For a list of `n` elements, there are `2^n` sublists, including the empty list and the full list. An example rule set shows a `Household.members` list of `Person` objects and a `memberCombinations` attribute derived with `sublists` to generate all combinations.

children.title: sum
children.summary: Defines the `sum` expression in Cúram rules. It calculates the numerical sum of a list of `Number` values and returns `0` for an empty list. The section notes that lists are typically supplied by `fixedlist` or `dynamiclist` and includes XML examples showing `sum` used to derive a person’s `netWorth` from a fixed list and `totalAssets` from a dynamic list.

children.title: this
children.summary: Explains the `this` expression in Cúram rule sets: it refers to the current Rule Object, like Java `this`. The example shows using `<this/>` inside `create` elements to pass the current `Person` as the owner when constructing `Pet` objects, producing a fixed list of two pets (`Skippy` and `Lassie`) for each `Person`.

children.title: Timeline
children.summary: Explains the Timeline expression, which creates a timeline of values valid from specified dates. It notes that a timeline must include a value from the start of time (null date), either via the optional initialvalue element or by including an Interval with a null start date; otherwise runtime evaluation errors occur. Includes XML examples showing a numeric timeline using initialvalue and a string timeline relying on intervals.

children.title: timelineoperation
children.summary: Explains the `timelineoperation` expression, which reassembles repeated child-expression results into a `Timeline` by evaluating at changing dates. It works with `intervalvalue`: `intervalvalue` extracts the value for the current date from an input timeline and queues future change dates, while `timelineoperation` reruns the child for each date. Notes that wrapping timeline inputs is usually required; otherwise the result is constant over time. Includes examples of computing total asset value and a validation error caused by forgetting `intervalvalue`.

children.title: true
children.summary: Explains the `true` expression in Cúram CER rules, which evaluates to the Boolean constant `true`, with XML examples showing direct use and negation via `<not><true/></not>`. Also introduces `XmlMessage`, used to create localizable messages from free-form XML content; the literal XML becomes the message, except `<replace>` elements are substituted with their contained expressions. Notes that `replace` is only simple token substitution and suggests `ResourceMessage` for more flexible messaging.

children.title: XmlMessage
children.summary: XmlMessage creates a localizable Message from free-form XML. The message is the literal XML content, except any replace element is evaluated and substituted with its contained expression. It is intended as a simple token-replacement mechanism; for more complex formatting or editable text, use ResourceMessage instead. The section also notes a behavior change from Cúram V6: whitespace is no longer trimmed (comments removed), with an environment variable available to restore trimming. Examples show empty, HTML-formatted, and token-replaced messages.

Alphabetical reference for CER expressions from abstract through XmlMessage. It defines core expression semantics, typing, error behavior, and usage notes, with XML examples for each. Covered topics include markers and constants (abstract, specified, true, Date, String, Code), logic and comparison (all, any, compare, choose), arithmetic and string/message building (arithmetic, concat, ResourceMessage, XmlMessage), object/list creation and selection (create, current, this, singleitem, sort, removeduplicates, sublists, sum), and timeline handling (Timeline, timelineoperation).

Overview of CER expressions in Cúram, organized both alphabetically and by function. It introduces attribute derivations and special marker expressions, then groups expressions for Boolean logic, value comparison, constants, conditional logic, list aggregations/transformations, localizable messages, numerical calculations, references, creation/retrieval, Java callouts, markers, timelines, and product delivery eligibility/entitlement. Also points to the full alphabetical listing with definitions, typing, error behavior, and examples.

children.title: Annotations
Full Alphabetical Listing of Annotations
ActiveInEditSuccessionSetPopulation
children.summary: Lists CER rule-set annotations and their purpose. It explains that annotations are extra metadata available to clients but do not affect CER calculation behavior, and can be attached to rule sets, classes, attributes, or expressions with validation limits. The section alphabetically catalogs annotations; for ActiveInEditSuccessionSetPopulation it only refers readers to “Configuring Advisor.” It also notes EditorMetadata is editor-maintained and rule-set only, Indexed is deprecated/backward compatibility only, and Label provides localized descriptions for rule items.

children.title: Display
children.summary: This section lists CER annotations and explains that annotations are extra metadata affecting client behavior, not CER calculations. It describes where annotations can be applied (rule set, rule class, rule attribute, expression), notes that each annotation type can appear only once per item, and provides an alphabetical listing. For the “Display” annotation specifically, it points to separate documentation in Developing with Eligibility and Entitlement by Using Cúram Express Rules.

children.title: DisplaySubscreen
children.summary: This section is part of the CER annotations reference. It lists available annotations and briefly describes each, including that annotations add metadata without affecting CER calculations. For DisplaySubscreen specifically, it contains only a pointer to “Developing with Eligibility and Entitlement by Using Cúram Express Rules,” with no further technical details here. Useful if you want the annotation index or need to find where DisplaySubscreen is documented in detail.

children.title: EditorMetadata
children.summary: EditorMetadata is a CER annotation that stores diagram information for a rule set and is maintained automatically by the CER editor. It can be specified only on a rule set, not on rule classes, attributes, or expressions. This section appears in the alphabetical listing of annotations and is useful if you need to understand how CER editor metadata is handled or where this annotation is valid.

children.title: Indexed
children.summary: This section lists CER annotations and explains their purpose and placement. It notes annotations are metadata that do not affect CER calculation behavior, can be attached to rule sets, classes, attributes, and expressions, and each annotation type can appear only once per item. It highlights specific annotations including Label, EditorMetadata, primary, Indexed, and several business-specific ones. Of note, Indexed is deprecated and kept only for backward compatibility. Examples show XML usage for Label and primary annotations.

Alphabetical reference of CER annotations. It explains that annotations are client-facing metadata, not used in CER calculation, and may be attached to rule sets, rule classes, rule attributes, or expressions, with each annotation type allowed only once per item. This section lists annotations such as ActiveInEditSuccessionSetPopulation, Display, DisplaySubscreen, EditorMetadata, Indexed, Label, Legislation, SuccessionSetPopulation, primary, relatedActiveInEditSuccessionSet, relatedEvidence, relatedSuccessionSet, and tags, often pointing to other docs. It includes a detailed Label description and XML examples for Label and primary.

Explains CER annotations as client-facing metadata that do not affect calculations. They can be attached to rule sets, classes, attributes, or expressions, with each annotation type allowed only once per item. Lists available annotations alphabetically, including those documented elsewhere. Key details covered here include EditorMetadata (editor-maintained rule-set diagram info), Indexed (obsolete), Label (localized descriptions with XML examples and resource-store behavior), primary (declares the primary class attribute), and tags.

Covers the CER XML Dictionary for rule sets, classes, attributes, expressions, and annotations. It explains rule-set structure and XSD constraints, include statements for splitting/reusing rule sets (RelativePath and Classpath, recursive Ruleset includes), rule class initialization vs calculated attributes and ordering rules, and attribute type/derivation requirements. Also summarizes the expression catalog by category. Annotation support is described as non-calculating metadata, including EditorMetadata, Indexed, Label, primary, and tags, with examples and placement rules.

children.title: 1.9 Useful List Operations
children.summary: Explains safe Java list operations available in CER property expressions for `java.util.List`. It lists exposed methods (`contains`, `containsAll`, `get`, `indexOf`, `isEmpty`, `lastIndexOf`, `size`, `subList`) and excludes unsafe or mutating ones. Gives brief JavaDoc-style definitions for `isEmpty`, `size`, `get(int)`, and `contains(Object)`, noting `get` uses zero-based indexing and CER numbers may need `intValue()`. Includes XML examples showing list-based derivations like checking children, counting them, and accessing the second child.

children.title: 1.10 Using CER with the Datastore
DataStoreRuleObjectCreator Example
children.summary: Explains how DataStoreRuleObjectCreator maps Datastore records into CER rule objects by recursively traversing descendants from a root record and doing natural mapping between entity/attribute names and rule classes/attributes. Special handling is shown for parentEntity (set to the parent rule object) and childEntities_<rule class name> (set to lists of child rule objects), with runtime errors on type mismatches. The example uses a household screening model (Application, Person, Income) to illustrate which records are created or skipped and how values like firstName, type, and amount are populated.

Explains how DataStoreRuleObjectCreator converts Datastore records into CER rule objects by traversing from a root record through descendants and doing a natural mapping between entity/attribute names and rule classes/attributes. Special handling applies to parentEntity (set to the parent rule object) and childEntities_<rule class name> (set to lists of child rule objects), with runtime errors on type mismatches. Includes a household screening example showing Application records skipped, Person and Income objects created, and fields like firstName, type, and amount populated.

children.title: 1.11 CER Compliancy
CER public API
children.summary: This section explains CER public API compliance rules. It states that CER infrastructure exposes a public API usable in rule set tests and application code, and that IBM/Merative will not change or remove it without standard customer-impact handling. It warns not to implement CER Java interfaces or subclasses unless Javadoc explicitly allows it. It also says the included Javadoc is the only authoritative source for identifying API classes, interfaces, and methods, and notes that some public members are outside the official API.

children.title: CER expressions compliancy
children.summary: This section explains CER compliancy rules: only use documented public CER APIs and supported CER expressions; unsupported or experimental expressions in the schema must not be used. It also warns not to place custom classes in the `curam.creole` package, not to alter the included `RootRuleSet`, and notes that included rule sets may have separate restrictions. Finally, it summarizes CER database table restrictions, especially the `CREOLERuleSet` table and its limited DMX population conditions.

children.title: Compliancy for included rule sets
children.summary: Explains compliance limits for included CER rule sets. It says customers must avoid dependencies on non-public CER classes, interfaces, methods, or unsupported CER expressions, and must not place custom code in the curam.creole package unless Javadoc allows it. It notes that included rule sets may have component-specific customization restrictions, so related documentation should be checked. Also states that the RootRuleSet included with CER must not be altered by customers.

children.title: CER database tables compliancy
children.summary: CER database tables are generally internal and accessible only through the CER public API. The main exception is CREOLERuleSet, which can be populated via Data Manager DMX files only under strict conditions: the name must match the XML rule set name and ruleSetVersion must be null. DMX insertion skips some API validation and historical snapshot creation, so invalid rule sets may be stored and later fail validation; use the creole.validate.rulesets target after loading.

children.title: Dependency Manager compliancy
children.summary: Dependency Manager is described as internal to Cúram and inaccessible from custom code, with no supported customization. Its artifacts live in the curam.dependency package and sub-packages, which customers must not extend. The section lists Dependency Manager-owned tables (Dependency, PrecedentChangeSet, PrecedentChangeItem, PrecedentChangeSetBatchCtrl) and states they must not be customized or directly read/written. Initial population via DMX is not supported except for application-included DMX files, and their population must not be altered or omitted.

Guidelines for compliant CER development and upgrade safety. Covers use of example artifacts, CER public API restrictions, and how the included Javadoc defines the API; custom implementations and classes in `curam.creole` are disallowed unless explicitly permitted. Requires using only supported CER expressions and respecting restrictions on included rule sets, including `RootRuleSet`. Also details CER database table rules: most are API-only, while `CREOLERuleSet` can be loaded via DMX only under strict conditions and should be validated afterward. Includes Dependency Manager access/customization limits.

Overview of Cúram Express Rules (CER): a rules language, editor, test environment, and runtime for business calculations. Covers core terminology (rule classes, objects, attributes, expressions, rule sets, sessions), XML-based storage and validation, production vs stand-alone testing with stubbing, RuleDoc/SessionDoc/Coverage Tool, dynamic publication and dependency-driven recalculation, usage in Universal Access, Advisor, and eligibility engines, and guiding principles such as on-demand calculation, immutability, and no working storage. Also includes editor, XML dictionary, datastore/list handling, best practices, and compliancy rules.
